title: Hamilton G5 Ventilator – Flow Sensor Errors
description: Troubleshooting guide for Hamilton G5 ventilator flow sensor alarms, calibration failures, and inaccurate flow or tidal volume readings.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Transfer the patient to another ventilator if actively in use. Do not troubleshoot flow measurement issues while connected to a patient. Flow sensor errors directly affect delivered volume accuracy and patient safety.

steps.title: Identify the Exact Alarm Message
steps.instructions: Document the specific wording of the alarm. Document when it occurs (startup, during ventilation, during calibration). Determine whether it is intermittent or constant. This helps determine whether the issue is setup-related or hardware-related.

steps.title: Inspect the Flow Sensor for Moisture or Contamination
steps.instructions: The Hamilton G5 flow sensor is highly sensitive to condensation, secretions, nebulized medications, and improper cleaning. Remove the flow sensor from the circuit. Inspect visually for moisture or debris. If wet, dry completely per facility protocol. Replace with a known-good spare sensor if available. Moisture is one of the most common causes of flow sensor alarms.

steps.title: Verify Proper Sensor Orientation and Connection
steps.instructions: Confirm the flow sensor is installed in the correct direction. Ensure connectors are fully seated. Inspect the cable connection to the ventilator module. Check for bent pins or damaged connectors. Improper orientation or loose connections can generate false readings or calibration failures.

steps.title: Replace the Breathing Circuit
steps.instructions: A compromised circuit can cause leaks, resistance changes, and flow miscalculations. Replace the entire patient circuit with a new, dry circuit. Avoid reusing circuits with heavy condensation. Ensure all connections are tight. Reattempt calibration after replacement.

steps.title: Perform Flow Sensor Calibration
steps.instructions: Perform flow sensor calibration per facility procedure using the ventilator’s service or setup menu. Ensure no patient is connected during calibration. Ensure the circuit is dry and properly assembled. If calibration fails repeatedly with multiple sensors and circuits, the issue may be internal.

steps.title: Check for Excessive Condensation in the Expiratory Path
steps.instructions: Inspect the expiratory valve assembly, water traps, and heated wire circuit function. Drain any water traps. Excess moisture can affect measurement accuracy and trigger alarms.

steps.title: Inspect for Physical Damage
steps.instructions: Examine the sensor for cracks, warping, discoloration, and evidence of overheating. Replace if any damage is present.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Ventilator
steps.instructions: Turn the ventilator off properly. Wait 30 seconds. Restart and observe for recurrence. Intermittent software-related sensor detection issues may clear with a restart.

CCR.Complaint: Nursing reported ‘Flow Sensor Error’ alarm on Hamilton G5. Ventilator failed pre-use calibration.
CCR.Cause: Flow sensor found with internal condensation. Circuit also contained excessive moisture.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced flow sensor and breathing circuit. Performed successful flow sensor calibration. Ventilator passed operational check and returned to service.

title: Hamilton G5 Ventilator – Startup / Self-Test Failures
description: Troubleshooting guide for Hamilton G5 ventilator startup and self-test failures using logical external checks before internal escalation.

steps.title: Ensure the Patient Is Safely Supported
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot while connected to a patient. Transfer the patient to another ventilator before proceeding. Confirm alternative ventilation is stable. Patient safety is always the first step.

steps.title: Verify AC Power Source
steps.instructions: Confirm the power cord is fully seated at both the ventilator and wall outlet. Test the wall outlet with a known working device. Avoid power strips if possible—plug directly into a hospital-grade outlet. Confirm the power indicator LED illuminates when plugged in. Startup failures are frequently related to unstable or intermittent AC power.

steps.title: Check Battery Status
steps.instructions: Attempt startup on AC power with battery installed. If unit attempts to boot then shuts down, battery may be severely depleted or failed. Remove and reseat the battery if accessible without disassembly. Observe battery indicator messages during startup. A failing internal battery can interrupt self-test even when AC is connected.

steps.title: Verify Gas Supply Connections
steps.instructions: Confirm oxygen and air hoses are properly connected and fully seated. Ensure wall gas pressure is within normal range (typically ~50 psi in U.S. facilities). Swap gas hoses with known-good hoses if supply pressure alarms appear. Listen for leaks at quick-connect fittings. The G5 performs gas pressure checks during startup. Insufficient supply can stop the self-test.

steps.title: Inspect Patient Circuit and Flow Sensor
steps.instructions: Remove the patient circuit and attempt startup without the circuit attached. Inspect the flow sensor for moisture, contamination, or incorrect orientation. Ensure expiratory valve assembly is properly seated. Replace with known-good components if available. Improperly installed or contaminated components can trigger self-test failure.

steps.title: Observe and Document Error Messages
steps.instructions: Record exact wording of error messages or codes. Note whether failure occurs immediately or at a specific phase (e.g., leak test, valve test). Listen for abnormal sounds (clicking, repeated cycling, no compressor sound). Pattern recognition helps determine whether the issue is accessory-related or internal.

steps.title: Power Cycle Properly
steps.instructions: Turn unit fully off. Disconnect AC power for 60 seconds. Reconnect and attempt a clean startup. This allows internal capacitors and logic boards to fully reset.

steps.title: Inspect for Environmental Factors
steps.instructions: Check for excessive dust buildup around air intake filters. Ensure rear cooling vents are not obstructed. Confirm the unit is not overheated. Thermal or airflow restrictions may interrupt boot sequence.

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator fails startup self-test and displays system failure during boot.
CCR.Cause: Unit failed during valve calibration phase. Verified AC power, gas supply at 50 psi, replaced circuit and flow sensor with known-good components. Error persisted after power cycle.
CCR.Resolution: Removed from service. Labeled Out of Service. Sent to bench for internal evaluation and repair.

helpfulDetails: Whether unit fully powers on or shuts down

title: Hamilton C6 Ventilator Battery Won’t Hold Charge
description: Guide for troubleshooting Hamilton C6 ventilator battery that does not hold charge or charges poorly.

steps.title: Safety First – Patient Not on Battery
steps.instructions: Ensure the ventilated patient is on mains power or an alternative power source. Do not rely on a low or questionable battery during active ventilation.

steps.title: Verify Primary Power Connection
steps.instructions:
Confirm the ventilator is plugged into a properly grounded, hospital‑grade receptacle.
Make sure the AC mains power is present at the outlet (use a known good outlet tester).
Inspect the power cord and connector for damage or loose contacts.
Why: The ventilator only charges its batteries when connected to primary AC power. No mains means no charging regardless of battery condition.

steps.title: Check Ambient Conditions
steps.instructions: Ensure the ventilator and battery are not being charged at temperatures >43 °C (e.g., near heat sources or in direct sunlight). High temperature can prevent charging.

steps.title: Install and Seat Battery Correctly
steps.instructions:
Remove and re‑install the battery to verify it is properly seated and the lock is fully engaged.
Inspect battery contacts for debris or corrosion.

steps.title: Observe Battery Indicators
steps.instructions:
Review the battery charge indicator on the ventilator display.
If the battery reads empty or does not register, check for any Battery communication error alarms; these may indicate poor connection or failed battery detection.

steps.title: Swap With Known Good Battery
steps.instructions:
If a second battery is available, swap it in and test whether it charges and holds charge.
If the spare battery behaves normally, the original battery may have degraded.

steps.title: Battery Calibration/Service Hint
steps.instructions:
Some designs internally calibrate battery capacity over multiple charge/discharge cycles; if the battery has not been used or calibrated in a long time, run a few controlled cycles (connect to mains, allow full charge, disconnect, allow full discharge) with service oversight.
Refer to manufacturer for recommended calibration/test intervals.

CCR.Complaint: Unit displays poor battery charge retention, depletes quickly, and will not hold charge.
CCR.Cause: Verified AC power present; battery does not indicate full charge; spare battery also fails; charging does not appear to progress; ambient conditions within normal range.
CCR.Resolution: Removed battery from service; unit labeled Out of Service; vendor notified for battery replacement; scheduled for bench evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Final device status after battery removal

title: Hamilton C6 Ventilator - Transport Ventilator Troubleshooting
description: Step-by-step troubleshooting guide for Hamilton C6 ventilator in transport mode, emphasizing external checks and patient safety.

steps.title: Check Power Source
steps.instructions: Verify the internal battery is charged. Confirm AC power connection if in stationary use. Reason: Transport mode relies on battery; insufficient power prevents operation.

steps.title: Confirm Battery Status
steps.instructions: Look for battery indicator on screen. If battery low (<20%), replace or fully charge. Reason: Low battery may prevent ventilator from initiating transport mode.

steps.title: Inspect Gas Supply Connections
steps.instructions: Ensure oxygen and air cylinders are properly connected and valves open. Verify hoses are not kinked or leaking. Reason: Transport mode may rely on cylinders rather than wall supply.

steps.title: Verify Ventilator Mode and Settings
steps.instructions: Confirm ventilator is in the correct transport mode. Ensure settings match patient requirements (tidal volume, PEEP, FiO₂). Reason: Misconfigured modes can trigger alarms or fail to deliver breaths.

steps.title: Check Circuit and Patient Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect breathing circuit, filters, and endotracheal tube connection. Make sure no obstructions exist. Reason: Even during transport, ventilator cannot operate with disconnected or blocked circuits.

steps.title: Run System Self-Test (if safe)
steps.instructions: Perform a pre-use check following on-screen prompts. Reason: Confirms basic system function and identifies any internal errors without disassembly.

steps.title: Observe Alarm Messages
steps.instructions: Note any error codes or messages. Reason: These guide further action; some may indicate battery, gas, or circuit issues.

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator would not operate during patient transport; alarms displayed “Battery Low” and “No Volume Delivered.”
CCR.Cause: Battery fully discharged; transport mode could not initiate.
CCR.Resolution: Battery replaced and fully charged; pre-use check passed; device confirmed functional in transport mode.

title: Hamilton G5 Ventilator – Oxygen Blending / FiO₂ Mismatch
description: Troubleshooting guide for Hamilton G5 ventilator FiO₂ mismatch, high/low oxygen alarms, and oxygen blending inconsistencies.

steps.title: Confirm Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the ventilator is actively supporting a patient, move the patient to another ventilator before troubleshooting. Do not attempt oxygen delivery troubleshooting while the device is in clinical use. This prevents unintended hypoxia or hyperoxia during testing.

steps.title: Verify Wall Gas Supply
steps.instructions: Confirm oxygen and air hoses are connected to the correct wall outlets. Check that both supplies are fully inserted and locked. Confirm wall pressure is within normal range (typically ~50 psi in U.S. facilities). If available, connect to a known-good outlet. The G5 blends oxygen and medical air internally. If one source is absent, low, or cross-connected, the delivered FiO₂ will not match the set value.

steps.title: Inspect Gas Hoses and DISS Fittings
steps.instructions: Check hoses for kinks, crushing, or damage. Inspect DISS fittings for debris or worn washers. Swap hoses with known-good hoses if uncertainty exists. A partially restricted air line, for example, can result in higher-than-set FiO₂.

steps.title: Check for Crossed Gas Lines
steps.instructions: Confirm oxygen hose is connected to the oxygen inlet. Confirm air hose is connected to the air inlet. Trace hoses physically from wall to ventilator if necessary. Crossed gas lines are a common external cause of FiO₂ mismatch.

steps.title: Run the Pre-Use Check / Calibration
steps.instructions: Perform a full system check per facility protocol. Allow the ventilator to complete oxygen sensor calibration. Observe if any gas supply or blending-related warnings appear. If the oxygen sensor calibration fails, this may indicate sensor drift or internal issue.

steps.title: Verify Oxygen Sensor Status
steps.instructions: Check oxygen sensor age and service interval. If the sensor is near end-of-life, replace per facility policy. After replacement, repeat calibration and verify stability. A drifting or expired oxygen sensor can falsely indicate mismatch even when blending is correct.

steps.title: Compare with External Oxygen Analyzer (If Available)
steps.instructions: Connect an external calibrated oxygen analyzer to the inspiratory limb. Compare displayed FiO₂ with ventilator reading. If external analyzer matches the ventilator → likely gas supply issue upstream. External analyzer disagrees with ventilator → suspect internal sensor or calibration issue. This step helps differentiate measurement error from true blending error.

steps.title: Check for Active Alarms or Messages
steps.instructions: Document any of the following: Low O₂ supply alarm, Air supply alarm, FiO₂ out-of-range, Calibration failure. Alarm behavior can help determine whether the issue is supply-related, sensor-related, or blending-related.

steps.title: Observe Stability Over Time
steps.instructions: Set a fixed FiO₂ (e.g., 50%). Monitor reading for drift. Check if fluctuations correlate with supply pressure changes. Intermittent drift may suggest unstable supply pressure rather than internal failure.

CCR.Complaint: Respiratory therapist reported that the ventilator was set to 40% FiO₂ but displayed 55% and triggered high oxygen alarm.
CCR.Cause: Air hose was partially kinked behind the equipment boom, restricting air supply and causing higher-than-set oxygen concentration.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced air hose, verified supply pressures, recalibrated oxygen sensor, and confirmed FiO₂ accuracy with external analyzer. Ventilator passed system check and returned to service.

title: Hamilton C6 Ventilator Touchscreen Lag / Freeze
description: Troubleshooting guide for touchscreen lag, freezes, and unresponsive menus on the Hamilton C6 ventilator.

steps.title: Check Power Source
steps.instructions: Verify AC mains connection or battery level. Low voltage or unstable power can cause the touchscreen to lag.

steps.title: Inspect Cables and Connectors
steps.instructions: Ensure AC power cord, battery contacts, and any USB/data ports are fully seated. Look for bent pins, corrosion, or frayed cables that could affect system stability.

steps.title: Confirm Environmental Conditions
steps.instructions: Ensure the ventilator is not exposed to excessive heat, direct sunlight, or high humidity, which can slow touch response. Remove any dust, liquid, or debris from the touchscreen surface.

steps.title: Restart the Device
steps.instructions: Power down the ventilator completely. Wait 30–60 seconds and power back on. Observe if touchscreen responsiveness improves.

steps.title: Check for Software Updates
steps.instructions: If safe to do so, verify the device firmware version. Outdated firmware can cause UI lag; note version for service records.

steps.title: Reduce System Load
steps.instructions: Close non-essential menus or monitoring windows if the device allows. Excessive simultaneous monitoring can temporarily slow touchscreen response.

steps.title: Observe for Patterned Behavior
steps.instructions: Note if freezing occurs with certain menus, alarms, or settings. Document times, duration, and any error messages for service escalation.

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator touchscreen is slow and sometimes freezes during normal operation.
CCR.Cause: Observed lag and unresponsive touch panels even after verifying power, connections, and environmental conditions.
CCR.Resolution: Device powered down, restarted, monitored; issue persists. Device labeled out of service and sent for repair.

helpfulDetails: AC outlet tested and confirmed stable

title: Hamilton C3 Ventilator Pre-Use Check Failures
description: Guide for troubleshooting Hamilton C3 ventilator pre-use check failures, focusing on external checks before internal issues.

steps.title: Verify Power Source
steps.instructions: Ensure the ventilator is connected to a live outlet or fully charged battery. Confirm the line voltage matches specifications. Why: Inadequate or unstable power can prevent successful pre-use checks.

steps.title: Check Battery and Battery Seating
steps.instructions: Remove and reseat the battery module. Inspect for corrosion or loose connections. If using external power, ensure battery bypass is properly engaged. Why: The ventilator will fail checks if it cannot verify battery status.

steps.title: Inspect Circuit and Humidifier Connections
steps.instructions: Ensure patient circuit is properly attached and no leaks are present. Check that all tubing, sensors, and filters are correctly seated. Why: Disconnected or misconnected circuits can trigger pre-use alarms.

steps.title: Check Flow Sensor / CO₂ Module (if applicable)
steps.instructions: Ensure modules are plugged in and recognized by the system. Look for any warning LEDs or disconnected cables. Why: Missing or unrecognized modules will halt pre-use testing.

steps.title: Examine Filters and Water Traps
steps.instructions: Ensure airway filters and water traps are installed and not saturated. Replace if needed. Why: Blocked sensors or saturated filters can cause check failures.

steps.title: Restart Ventilator
steps.instructions: Power down completely, wait 30 seconds, then power on. Observe self-test sequence carefully. Why: Temporary software glitches or communication errors can sometimes be cleared with a restart.

steps.title: Review Error Codes / Alarms
steps.instructions: Note any displayed messages and consult internal code definitions. Why: Error codes often identify the subsystem causing pre-use check failure.

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator failed pre-use check during startup; displayed “Check Failure” error.
CCR.Cause: Circuit not properly connected; battery seating verified; filters installed correctly.
CCR.Resolution: Reconnected circuit, reseated battery, replaced saturated filter; pre-use check passed after restart.

title: Hamilton C3 Ventilator - Touchscreen Lag or Freeze
description: Guide for troubleshooting delayed or unresponsive touchscreen on Hamilton C3 ventilators, focusing on external and software checks before hardware intervention.

steps.title: Verify Power Supply
steps.instructions: Ensure the ventilator is connected to a verified working AC outlet. Check the battery level; low battery can cause UI lag. Why: Inconsistent power can cause touchscreen responsiveness issues.

steps.title: Reboot the Ventilator
steps.instructions: Power off the unit using proper shutdown procedure. Wait 30 seconds, then power back on. Why: Temporary software glitches are often resolved by a clean restart.

steps.title: Check External Connections
steps.instructions: Disconnect any non-essential peripherals (USB drives, external sensors, printer). Confirm that circuit connections and breathing system components are properly seated. Why: Conflicts with external devices can cause system lag.

steps.title: Inspect Touchscreen Surface
steps.instructions: Clean the screen with a soft, lint-free cloth. Avoid harsh chemicals. Check for moisture, condensation, or physical damage. Why: Debris or moisture can interfere with touch detection.

steps.title: Monitor Software Status
steps.instructions: Check for on-screen messages indicating software updates or errors. Verify that the system is running the latest approved firmware. Why: Outdated or interrupted updates can reduce touchscreen performance.

steps.title: Reset Non-volatile Memory (if instructed by software)
steps.instructions: Follow the C3 software prompts for safe reset of touchscreen parameters. Why: Certain UI parameters can become corrupted and resetting may restore normal operation.

CCR.Complaint: Clinician reports the ventilator touchscreen is slow or unresponsive.
CCR.Cause: Observed intermittent lag, temporary freezes, and unregistered inputs; power and connections verified.
CCR.Resolution: Ventilator rebooted; peripherals disconnected; screen cleaned; firmware verified. Issue persists → unit labeled Out of Service and sent for service.

helpfulDetails: Final device status: Out of Service

title: Hamilton MR1 Ventilator - Flow Delivery Problems
description: Guide for troubleshooting flow delivery issues on the Hamilton MR1 ventilator, focusing on external checks before internal repair.

steps.title: Ensure Safe Environment
steps.instructions: Confirm patient is supported on an alternative ventilator or manual bagging if necessary. Do not troubleshoot flow issues on an actively ventilated patient without backup.

steps.title: Check Power Supply
steps.instructions: Verify AC mains and battery status. Ensure the ventilator is fully powered and not in low-battery or power-failure mode.

steps.title: Verify Circuit Connections
steps.instructions: Inspect inspiratory and expiratory limbs for secure connections. Confirm the patient circuit is assembled according to MR1 guidelines.

steps.title: Check for Obstructions
steps.instructions: Examine filters, humidifiers, and bacterial/viral filters for blockage. Remove or replace any suspect filter and verify flow.

steps.title: Inspect Sensors and Tubing
steps.instructions: Ensure flow sensors are properly installed and not wet, dirty, or kinked. Verify that the flow sensor calibration or zeroing step completed successfully.

steps.title: Check for Leaks
steps.instructions: Inspect patient circuit and endotracheal tube connections for leaks. Confirm cuff inflation if using an ETT or tracheostomy.

steps.title: Review Ventilator Settings
steps.instructions: Confirm tidal volume, inspiratory flow, and mode settings are appropriate. Compare set vs. delivered values on the MR1 display.

steps.title: Run Built-In Self-Test
steps.instructions: Perform the MR1’s ventilator self-test. Observe any error codes, especially those related to flow sensors or pneumatic systems.

steps.title: Swap Accessories
steps.instructions: If available, swap the circuit and flow sensor with a known working set to isolate the problem.

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator not delivering expected flow; alarms “Low Flow” triggered.
CCR.Cause: Flow sensor was wet and partially obstructed; circuit had minor leak at connection.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned and reinstalled flow sensor; checked circuit connections; confirmed proper tidal volume delivery on test lung.

helpfulDetails: Filters inspected and replaced if needed

title: Hamilton MR1 Ventilator - Battery / Power Failure
description: Guide for troubleshooting Hamilton MR1 ventilator battery or power failures focusing on external checks before internal repair.

steps.title: Confirm AC Power Availability
steps.instructions: Verify the wall outlet is live using a known-working device or outlet tester. Check that the MR1 power cord is fully inserted and undamaged. Why: An MR1 may fail to power on if the outlet is dead or cord is damaged.

steps.title: Inspect the Power Cord and Adapter
steps.instructions: Look for frays, cuts, or bent prongs on the power cord. Swap with a spare compatible power cord if available. Why: External cord damage is a common cause of power issues.

steps.title: Check Battery Installation
steps.instructions: Ensure the battery pack is fully seated in the ventilator battery compartment. Verify the battery is the correct type for the MR1. Why: Loose or incompatible batteries can prevent startup or trigger low-battery alarms.

steps.title: Charge the Battery
steps.instructions: Connect the ventilator to AC power and allow the battery to charge for the recommended time (per manufacturer guidelines). Observe whether the charging indicator illuminates. Why: A completely drained battery may prevent power-on until partially charged.

steps.title: Perform a Soft Reset / Power Cycle
steps.instructions: If the ventilator has a soft reset or restart procedure, perform it. Disconnect from AC power, remove the battery, wait 30 seconds, reinstall battery, and reconnect AC power. Why: Power glitches can sometimes be cleared by a complete cycle.

steps.title: Swap Batteries (if available)
steps.instructions: Test the ventilator with a known-good battery pack. Why: Confirms whether the original battery is faulty.

steps.title: Observe for Alarms / Error Messages
steps.instructions: Note any battery-related alerts or error codes. Why: Error codes can help differentiate between battery failure, charger fault, or internal power module issues.

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator fails to power on; battery warning displayed.
CCR.Cause: Observed that battery pack was fully drained and AC outlet was functional. Power cord intact. Battery failed to charge after extended connection.
CCR.Resolution: Removed battery; installed a known-good battery. Verified ventilator powers on and charges correctly. Labeled old battery as defective for replacement.

helpfulDetails: Outlet verified and tested with a known device

title: Hamilton C3 Battery Won’t Hold Charge
description: Guide for troubleshooting a Hamilton‑C3 ventilator battery that won’t hold charge or discharges rapidly.

steps.title: Confirm Connection to Primary Power (AC/DC)
steps.instructions: Ensure the ventilator is connected to AC mains or appropriate DC power and verify the charging indicator shows a charge in progress. The batteries charge only when primary power is present; if there’s no proper connection to mains or DC, the battery won’t charge fully.

steps.title: Check Battery Charge Indicator Icons
steps.instructions: Look at the display icons for battery status (green = charged; orange/green = partial; crossed‑out = discharged/defective). A crossed‑out battery symbol often means the battery is discharged or faulty.

steps.title: Verify Battery Age and Usage History
steps.instructions: Batteries have a limited cycle life (typically a few hundred cycles for medical ventilator Li‑ion cells). If the battery is old or has been deeply discharged repeatedly, capacity will degrade. Hospitals often find every 2–3 years battery life drops significantly; a failing pack won’t hold charge even after charging.

steps.title: Ensure Proper Charging Period
steps.instructions: Charge on primary power for several hours (4+ hours) to bring battery up to 80‑100% per the manual before testing how long it holds. Short charge cycles may not allow full capacity.

steps.title: Swap or Test With Second Battery (if installed)
steps.instructions: If the C3 has two batteries installed, swap them to see if one holds charge better. A rapid drop in available time on battery suggests the pack itself is worn.

steps.title: Check Operating Environment
steps.instructions: High temperatures during charging or storage and prolonged idle periods without use can degrade battery performance. Store spare batteries in cool, dry conditions and cycle them monthly.

CCR.Complaint: Battery will not retain charge; battery time drops to near zero shortly after full charge.
CCR.Cause: Battery status indicator shows degraded battery despite being connected to primary power for >4 hours; battery is likely worn out.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced ventilator battery with a new Hamilton Medical Li‑ion battery pack; verified charging and normal battery run‑time.

helpfulDetails: Final device status after battery replacement

title: Hamilton MR1 Ventilator - Alarm Malfunctions
description: Guide for troubleshooting false, intermittent, or misconfigured alarms on the Hamilton MR1 ventilator.

steps.title: Check Power Supply
steps.instructions: Verify the ventilator is connected to a properly functioning outlet. Inspect for any battery low warnings. Why: Insufficient or fluctuating power can cause false alarms or silence critical alerts.

steps.title: Inspect Ventilator Cables and Connectors
steps.instructions: Examine all patient circuit connections and sensors (pressure, flow, O₂ sensors). Ensure cables are seated fully and not damaged. Why: Loose or damaged sensors commonly cause alarm malfunctions.

steps.title: Verify Alarm Settings
steps.instructions: Confirm that high/low limits for pressure, volume, rate, and SpO₂ are set correctly. Reset to default settings if uncertain. Why: Misconfigured alarms often appear as “malfunctions” but are actually settings issues.

steps.title: Check for Sensor Obstructions or Contamination
steps.instructions: Inspect airway sensors and flow sensors for moisture, secretions, or kinks. Clean or replace disposable sensors per protocol. Why: Contaminated or blocked sensors can trigger false alarms.

steps.title: Perform a Self-Test / Diagnostic
steps.instructions: Use the ventilator’s built-in self-test function before connecting to a patient. Observe if alarms trigger correctly during test. Why: This helps isolate whether the issue is software/electronics or patient circuit-related.

steps.title: Check for Software Updates / Logs
steps.instructions: Review device logs for error codes or unusual alarm events. Verify firmware is up-to-date according to manufacturer recommendations. Why: Outdated software or known firmware bugs can affect alarm performance.

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator alarms are triggering incorrectly and intermittently.
CCR.Cause: Observed loose O₂ sensor connector and high alarm threshold misconfiguration.
CCR.Resolution: Reconnected sensor, reset alarm limits, and ran self-test; alarms functioned normally.

helpfulDetails: Device labeled Out of Service if unresolved

title: Hamilton C1 Ventilator - Ventilation Errors
description: Guide to troubleshooting ventilation alarms and errors on the Hamilton C1 ventilator with step-by-step external checks before escalation.

steps.title: Verify Power and Battery Status
steps.instructions: Ensure the ventilator is connected to a working AC outlet or that the internal battery is charged. Power interruptions can trigger ventilation alarms. Check the display for battery warnings or AC power symbols.

steps.title: Check Breathing Circuit Connections
steps.instructions: Confirm that inspiratory and expiratory limbs are securely connected to the ventilator and patient interface. Loose tubing can cause pressure and volume errors.

steps.title: Inspect Filters and Humidifiers
steps.instructions: Verify that bacterial/viral filters are not blocked. Check the humidifier and water traps for obstruction or overfilling. Resistance in these components can trigger pressure alarms.

steps.title: Examine Patient Interface (ET Tube or Mask)
steps.instructions: Ensure the endotracheal tube or mask is patent. Check for kinks, obstructions, or accidental extubation. Suction if secretions are present, as they can prevent volume delivery.

steps.title: Review Ventilator Settings
steps.instructions: Verify that tidal volume, inspiratory time, PEEP, and FiO₂ are appropriate for the patient. Confirm mode selection matches patient condition (e.g., volume vs. pressure control). Incorrect settings may cause alarms even when equipment functions properly.

steps.title: Perform Quick Self-Test
steps.instructions: Navigate the Hamilton C1 system menu and run the built-in system check. Observe for any error codes that identify sensor or circuit problems.

steps.title: Swap Consumables if Needed
steps.instructions: Replace filters, tubing, or disposable sensors if the problem persists. This helps isolate whether the error is due to consumables rather than the ventilator itself.

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator triggers high-pressure alarm and fails to deliver set tidal volume.
CCR.Cause: Checked all external connections, tubing, filters, and patient interface; all appeared functional. Settings verified. Device still alarms during operation.
CCR.Resolution: Ventilator removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent to the manufacturer for repair. Patient moved to a backup ventilator.

helpfulDetails: Battery status and AC power checked
Any abnormal sounds or display messages noted

title: Hamilton C1 Ventilator - Battery / Power Problems
description: Guide for troubleshooting battery and power issues on the Hamilton C1 ventilator, focusing on external checks before internal repair.

steps.title: Verify AC Power Source
steps.instructions: Ensure the ventilator is plugged into a verified working outlet. Check for tripped circuit breakers or switched-off power strips. Why: A faulty power source is the most common cause of power-related issues.

steps.title: Inspect Power Cord and Connector
steps.instructions: Look for damage, fraying, or bent pins. Re-seat the cord firmly into the ventilator and outlet. Why: Loose or damaged cords can prevent proper charging or startup.

steps.title: Check Battery Installation
steps.instructions: Remove and reinstall the battery pack, ensuring it clicks fully into place. Inspect the battery contacts for corrosion or debris. Why: Improper seating or dirty contacts can prevent charging.

steps.title: Test Battery Charge Status
steps.instructions: Use the ventilator’s status screen to check battery level and health. If the battery shows very low charge despite being on AC power, it may have reached end-of-life. Why: Batteries degrade over time and may no longer hold sufficient charge.

steps.title: Attempt Power-On Without Battery
steps.instructions: With AC power connected, remove the battery and attempt to power on. Why: Confirms if the problem is battery-related versus AC/power circuitry.

steps.title: Swap with a Known Good Battery (if available)
steps.instructions: Insert a verified working battery to see if the issue persists. Why: Helps isolate the problem to the battery or internal power system.

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator fails to power on, battery error displayed.
CCR.Cause: Battery pack not charging; contacts clean but battery shows 0% charge.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced battery with verified functional unit; ventilator powered on successfully.

helpfulDetails: Any unusual sounds or indicator lights during power-on
Status of ventilator during troubleshooting (powered on/off)

title: Hamilton C1 Ventilator - Pre-Use Check Failures
description: Guide for troubleshooting pre-use checkout failures on the Hamilton C1 ventilator, focusing on external causes before assuming internal failure.

steps.title: Verify Power Source
steps.instructions: Ensure the ventilator is plugged into a verified, live outlet. Confirm the battery is charged if running on internal battery. Why: Low or unstable power often causes pre-use check failures.

steps.title: Check Main Power Switch & Startup Sequence
steps.instructions: Power on the ventilator and observe the startup screen. Ensure no buttons are stuck or pressed during startup. Why: Improper startup or button press can interrupt self-tests.

steps.title: Inspect Breathing Circuit & Patient Interfaces
steps.instructions: Confirm that circuit tubing, filters, humidifiers, and sensors are properly connected. Remove any circuit accessories and try running the pre-use check again. Why: Circuit misconfiguration or disconnected sensors can cause automated checks to fail.

steps.title: Check Oxygen and Air Supply (if applicable)
steps.instructions: Verify oxygen and air lines are connected to the ventilator and supply pressures are within operational range. Ensure no kinks or obstructions in supply hoses. Why: Ventilator self-test includes gas flow verification.

steps.title: Examine Sensors and Alarms
steps.instructions: Look for any disconnected, loose, or damaged pressure, flow, or oxygen sensors. Inspect for visible contamination or moisture in sensor ports. Why: Faulty sensors prevent successful pre-use checks.

steps.title: Run Diagnostics / System Test
steps.instructions: Follow on-screen prompts for self-test or pre-use check. Record any error codes displayed. Why: Codes give insight into which system component is causing failure.

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator fails pre-use checkout; displays ‘Self-Test Failed’ message.
CCR.Cause: Observed loose oxygen sensor and partially connected breathing circuit tubing during pre-use check.
CCR.Resolution: Reconnected all sensors and breathing circuit components, retested pre-use check. If still failing, ventilator marked Out of Service for service evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Final device status labeled (Out of Service if unresolved)

title: Hamilton T1 Ventilator - Battery Not Charging or Shortened Transport Runtime
description: Troubleshooting guide for Hamilton T1 ventilator battery charging issues and reduced runtime during transport.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot while the ventilator is in use on a patient. Move the patient to a stable, alternate ventilator before proceeding. Ensure therapy continuity at all times.

steps.title: Verify AC Power Source
steps.instructions: Confirm the ventilator is plugged into a known working outlet. Test the outlet with another device. Check for switched outlets or tripped breakers. This ensures the device is receiving proper external power.

steps.title: Check Power Supply and Cord
steps.instructions: Inspect the AC power cord and power supply brick. Look for fraying, bent prongs, or loose connections. Ensure the cord is fully seated in both the outlet and ventilator. Damaged or loose power components can prevent charging.

steps.title: Confirm Charging Indicator Status
steps.instructions: Power on the ventilator and check for battery/charging indicators. Verify the device recognizes AC power. Look for charging icons or status messages. Absence of indicators may point to power supply or internal charging issues.

steps.title: Inspect Battery Installation
steps.instructions: If removable batteries are used, ensure batteries are properly seated and latched. Remove and reseat batteries to confirm good connection. Poor battery contact can prevent charging.

steps.title: Check for Multiple Battery Configuration Issues
steps.instructions: If the unit has multiple batteries installed, remove one battery at a time and test charging. Identify if one battery is faulty and affecting the system. A single bad battery can impact overall charging performance.

steps.title: Evaluate Battery Age and Condition
steps.instructions: Review battery service history. Older batteries (typically >2–3 years) may have reduced capacity. Swollen or overheated batteries should be removed immediately. Battery degradation is a common cause of shortened runtime.

steps.title: Perform a Functional Runtime Check
steps.instructions: Fully charge the ventilator, then run it on battery power. Observe how long it operates compared to expected runtime. Note any rapid drop in battery percentage. This helps confirm whether the issue is capacity-related.

steps.title: Check for Alarms or Error Messages
steps.instructions: Review the screen for battery or power-related alarms. Low battery warnings. Charging faults. These can help identify whether the issue is external or internal.

steps.title: Test with Known Good Components (If Available)
steps.instructions: Swap with a known good battery. Use a known good power supply. This helps isolate whether the issue is with the battery or charger.

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator reported not charging and losing power quickly during patient transport.
CCR.Cause: Battery found to have reduced capacity and failed runtime test; charging confirmed functional with alternate battery.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced faulty battery and verified proper charging and expected runtime on AC and battery power.

helpfulDetails: Whether known good battery/power supply was tested
Final device status (returned to service or sent for repair)

title: Hamilton T1 Ventilator - Turbine Failure or Insufficient Pressure
description: Troubleshooting turbine failure or insufficient pressure on a Hamilton T1 ventilator.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Ensure the patient is removed and placed on an alternate ventilator. This prevents risk during troubleshooting of airflow or pressure issues.

steps.title: Check Power Source and Battery Status
steps.instructions: Confirm the ventilator is connected to AC power or has adequate battery charge. Low power can limit turbine performance and cause pressure issues.

steps.title: Inspect Air Inlet and Filter
steps.instructions: Check the turbine air intake filter for blockage, dust, or contamination. A clogged filter restricts airflow and reduces pressure output. Replace filter if dirty.

steps.title: Verify Circuit Setup
steps.instructions: Ensure the patient circuit is properly connected and not kinked or occluded. Look for water buildup, collapsed tubing, or incorrect circuit configuration. Restrictions can mimic turbine failure by limiting delivered pressure.

steps.title: Check for Leaks in the Circuit
steps.instructions: Inspect all connections (humidifier, filters, exhalation valve, mask/ET tube). Large leaks can prevent the ventilator from achieving target pressure.

steps.title: Observe Alarm Messages and Indicators
steps.instructions: Note any turbine-related alarms or pressure limit warnings. Listen for abnormal turbine sounds (e.g., grinding, no spin-up, inconsistent speed). Lack of normal turbine sound may indicate mechanical failure.

steps.title: Run Pre-Use Check / Self-Test
steps.instructions: Perform a system check if available. Failures during the test may help isolate turbine or flow generation issues.

steps.title: Check Oxygen Source and Settings
steps.instructions: Confirm oxygen supply (if connected) is functioning properly. While turbine-driven, improper gas supply or blending settings may affect performance.

steps.title: Restart the Ventilator
steps.instructions: Power cycle the unit to reset any temporary faults. Reassess if turbine operation returns to normal.

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator alarming for turbine failure and unable to maintain set pressure during transport.
CCR.Cause: Air intake filter found heavily obstructed, restricting turbine airflow and reducing pressure output.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced air intake filter, verified proper airflow, and confirmed normal pressure delivery during functional test.

helpfulDetails: AC power verified / battery status checked
Circuit inspected for kinks or occlusions
Final device status (returned to service or sent for repair)

title: Hamilton T1 Ventilator - Circuit Leak or Disconnect Alarms
description: Troubleshooting circuit leak or disconnect alarms on a Hamilton T1 ventilator by identifying external causes before internal faults.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Ensure the patient is supported on another ventilator if needed. Do not troubleshoot while the device is actively supporting a patient.

steps.title: Inspect the Entire Breathing Circuit
steps.instructions: Check all tubing from ventilator outlet to patient interface. Look for loose connections, disconnections, or cracks in the tubing. Even small gaps can trigger leak alarms.

steps.title: Check Patient Interface (ET Tube / Mask)
steps.instructions: Ensure proper connection at the airway. Verify cuff inflation if applicable. A leaking cuff is a common cause of persistent leak alarms.

steps.title: Verify Proper Circuit Assembly
steps.instructions: Confirm all components (filters, humidifier, water traps) are installed correctly. Missing or improperly seated components can create leaks.

steps.title: Inspect Filters and Water Traps
steps.instructions: Check for loose or improperly seated filters. Ensure water traps are fully closed and not cracked.

steps.title: Check for Excess Condensation
steps.instructions: Drain any accumulated water in the circuit. Condensation can interfere with flow and pressure readings.

steps.title: Run Pre-Use Check / Leak Test
steps.instructions: Perform the ventilator’s system check. This helps identify if the leak is internal or external. Ensure the circuit is capped properly during testing.

steps.title: Verify Alarm Settings and Sensitivity
steps.instructions: Check if alarm thresholds are set appropriately. Overly sensitive settings may trigger nuisance alarms.

steps.title: Swap with a Known-Good Circuit
steps.instructions: Replace the entire breathing circuit with a new or verified one. If the issue resolves, the original circuit was faulty.

steps.title: Check Flow Sensor and Connections
steps.instructions: Ensure the flow sensor is clean, properly connected, and not damaged. Misreading flow can mimic a leak condition.

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator alarming for circuit leak/disconnect during use.
CCR.Cause: Loose connection identified at expiratory limb and minor crack in breathing circuit tubing.
CCR.Resolution: Re-secured all connections, replaced breathing circuit, and verified proper operation with leak test.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (returned to service or sent for repair)

title: Hamilton T1 Ventilator - Pre-use Check Failures
description: Troubleshooting guide for Hamilton T1 ventilator pre-use check failures, focusing on circuit setup, sensors, and external causes.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Ensure the ventilator is not connected to a patient. Pre-use checks must be performed off-patient to avoid false failures and ensure patient safety.

steps.title: Verify proper circuit setup
steps.instructions: Confirm the breathing circuit is assembled correctly and matches the selected configuration (adult/pediatric, single/dual limb). Misconfigured circuits are one of the most common causes of failure.

steps.title: Check all circuit connections
steps.instructions: Inspect connections at the ventilator, humidifier, and patient wye. Loose or partially connected tubing can cause leaks and test failure.

steps.title: Inspect the circuit for damage or leaks
steps.instructions: Look for cracks, holes, or worn tubing. Replace the circuit if any damage is found.

steps.title: Confirm correct humidifier setup (if in use)
steps.instructions: Ensure the humidifier is properly connected and not introducing leaks. If unsure, temporarily remove the humidifier and rerun the test.

steps.title: Check and reseat flow sensor(s)
steps.instructions: Ensure the flow sensor is clean, dry, and properly connected. Moisture or debris can cause calibration or accuracy failures.

steps.title: Verify expiratory valve and filter condition
steps.instructions: Inspect for contamination, moisture, or blockage. Replace disposable components if needed.

steps.title: Ensure proper gas supply (if applicable)
steps.instructions: If using external oxygen, confirm the supply is connected and within normal pressure range. Inadequate supply can cause test failures.

steps.title: Restart the ventilator and rerun the pre-use check
steps.instructions: A reboot can clear temporary faults or incomplete initialization.

steps.title: Try a known-good circuit and components
steps.instructions: Swap with a verified working setup to isolate whether the issue is accessory-related.

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator fails pre-use check during setup; will not pass system test.
CCR.Cause: Leak detected due to loose circuit connection at expiratory limb (or contaminated flow sensor, or faulty circuit).
CCR.Resolution: Reseated all connections and replaced breathing circuit; pre-use check passed successfully.

helpfulDetails: Whether a known-good circuit was tested
Condition of flow sensor and expiratory valve
Any visible damage or moisture in components
Final device status (passed/failed and removed from service if applicable)

title: Hamilton T1 Ventilator - Oxygen Sensor Errors or Inaccuracy
description: Guide for troubleshooting oxygen sensor errors or inaccurate FiO2 readings on a Hamilton T1 ventilator.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot while the ventilator is connected to a patient. Move the patient to a confirmed working ventilator before proceeding.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact alarm or symptom (e.g., FiO2 mismatch, sensor error, drift). Compare displayed FiO2 with expected settings or an external oxygen analyzer if available. This helps determine if the issue is true inaccuracy or a display/calibration issue.

steps.title: Check Oxygen Supply Source
steps.instructions: Ensure oxygen supply is properly connected and turned on. Verify adequate pressure from wall supply or cylinder. Low or inconsistent supply can cause inaccurate readings.

steps.title: Inspect Gas Hoses and Connections
steps.instructions: Check for kinks, loose fittings, or leaks in the oxygen supply line. Ensure proper connection to the ventilator inlet. Leaks or restrictions can affect oxygen delivery and sensor readings.

steps.title: Perform Oxygen Sensor Calibration
steps.instructions: Run the ventilator’s oxygen calibration procedure per standard operation. Ensure calibration is done in the correct environment (typically room air or 100% O2 as required). Improper or skipped calibration is a common cause of inaccuracy.

steps.title: Verify Ambient Conditions
steps.instructions: Ensure the ventilator is not exposed to extreme humidity, temperature, or contaminants. Oxygen sensors can drift if environmental conditions are outside normal operating range.

steps.title: Inspect the Oxygen Sensor
steps.instructions: Check for expiration date (many O2 sensors have a limited lifespan). Look for signs of damage, corrosion, or moisture intrusion. Aging or contaminated sensors are a common failure point.

steps.title: Replace the Oxygen Sensor (if applicable)
steps.instructions: If calibration fails or readings remain inaccurate, replace the sensor with a known good one. Recalibrate after replacement. This is a standard field-level corrective action.

steps.title: Check for Condensation or Moisture
steps.instructions: Inspect internal tubing and sensor area (externally accessible areas only). Excess moisture can affect sensor accuracy. Allow system to dry if needed.

steps.title: Restart the Ventilator
steps.instructions: Power cycle the unit and allow it to complete self-test. Recheck oxygen readings after restart. This can clear temporary faults or calibration glitches.

steps.title: Run Pre-Use Check
steps.instructions: Perform a full system check to verify proper operation. Ensure no additional gas delivery or sensor-related errors are present.

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator displaying incorrect FiO2 values and intermittent oxygen sensor error alarm.
CCR.Cause: Oxygen sensor failed calibration and was found to be expired.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced oxygen sensor, performed calibration, and verified accurate FiO2 readings with external analyzer. Device passed pre-use check and returned to service.

title: Hamilton C1 Ventilator - Alarm: 231006 Oxygen supply failed
description: Troubleshooting Alarm 231006 Oxygen supply failed on the Hamilton C1 ventilator when low oxygen supply or O2 control flow may affect delivered FiO2.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Assess the patient immediately, confirm adequate ventilation and oxygenation, and transfer the patient to another ventilator or approved backup ventilation method if oxygen delivery is questionable.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the displayed alarm is Oxygen supply failed and verify Alarm ID 231006 if available in the event log or alarm history. Note whether the alarm occurs at startup, during use, during transport, or during high FiO2 demand.

steps.title: Confirm the Oxygen Source Is Available
steps.instructions: Check that the wall oxygen outlet is active, the oxygen source is not shut off, and any cylinder in use is open with adequate pressure. Try a known-good oxygen source if available.

steps.title: Check the O2 Hose
steps.instructions: Confirm the O2 hose is connected at both ends, inspect for damage, kinks, cracks, loose fittings, or pinched sections, and swap with a known-good O2 hose if available.

steps.title: Check the Oxygen Inlet Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect the ventilator oxygen inlet connection for loose seating, cross-threading, damaged threads, visible seal damage, or leaks. Reseat the connection and confirm it is secure.

steps.title: Check Oxygen Supply Pressure
steps.instructions: Verify that the wall source or cylinder regulator provides adequate pressure and does not drop under ventilator demand, especially during high FiO2 or high flow conditions.

steps.title: Inspect the O2 Inlet Filter
steps.instructions: Inspect the oxygen inlet filter if accessible per facility procedure. Look for contamination, moisture, debris, or blockage and replace or service only according to approved guidance.

steps.title: Rule Out Transport or Setup Issues
steps.instructions: Check for recent movement, cylinder changes, wrong gas outlet connection, unopened transport cylinder, or an O2 hose trapped behind equipment.

steps.title: Test With a Known-Good Oxygen Source and Hose
steps.instructions: Connect the ventilator to a verified oxygen source using a known-good O2 hose. If the alarm clears, correct the original external oxygen supply issue. If the alarm remains, external supply causes have likely been ruled out.

steps.title: Evaluate for Internal O2 Control or Flow Sensing Fault
steps.instructions: Remove the ventilator from patient use, label it Out of Service, document the external checks performed, and send the unit for bench evaluation or manufacturer service.

CCR.Complaint: Hamilton C1 ventilator reported Alarm 231006 Oxygen supply failed during use. Staff reported concern that delivered FiO2 may not match the set oxygen concentration.
CCR.Cause: Oxygen supply path issue suspected. Wall oxygen source, O2 hose, inlet connection, supply pressure, O2 inlet filter, and oxygen source availability were checked. Alarm persisted with known-good external oxygen supply, indicating likely internal O2 control or flow sensing fault.
CCR.Resolution: Removed ventilator from patient use, labeled Out of Service, and sent for bench evaluation/repair. Documented alarm behavior and external oxygen supply checks performed.

title: Hamilton C1 Ventilator - Alarm: 232007 Check flow sensor tubing
description: Troubleshooting Alarm: 232007 Check flow sensor tubing on the Hamilton C1 ventilator when flow sensor lines are disconnected, blocked, wet, reversed, kinked, or incorrectly connected, affecting flow and volume measurement reliability.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the ventilator from patient use if ventilation is unstable, flow readings are unreliable, or the alarm cannot be cleared quickly. Transfer the patient to another ventilator or approved manual ventilation method if needed.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the Hamilton C1 displays Alarm: 232007 Check flow sensor tubing. Observe whether the alarm occurs during startup, pre-use check, after circuit setup, during ventilation, or when the circuit is moved.

steps.title: Inspect the Flow Sensor Tubing Connections
steps.instructions: Check both small flow sensor pressure lines between the flow sensor and ventilator. Confirm both lines are fully seated at the ventilator ports and patient-side flow sensor.

steps.title: Check for Reversed Flow Sensor Tubing
steps.instructions: Verify the flow sensor tubing is connected in the correct orientation. Correct crossed, reversed, or incorrectly connected tubing before repeating required checks.

steps.title: Check for Kinked, Pinched, or Blocked Tubing
steps.instructions: Inspect the full length of both flow sensor tubing lines for sharp bends, compression, pinching, stretched tubing, trapped tubing, dried secretions, debris, or fluid blockage.

steps.title: Check for Moisture or Contamination
steps.instructions: Inspect the flow sensor tubing and flow sensor for water, condensation, secretions, cracks, contamination, or incorrect assembly. Replace wet, contaminated, or damaged components.

steps.title: Replace the Flow Sensor Tubing or Flow Sensor
steps.instructions: Install known-good compatible flow sensor tubing and a known-good compatible flow sensor if available. Confirm the alarm clears with known-good external flow sensing components.

steps.title: Repeat the Pre-Use Check or Self-Test
steps.instructions: Run the required Hamilton C1 pre-use check or self-test according to facility procedure. Confirm the alarm does not return and flow and volume readings are stable.

steps.title: Isolate Circuit-Related vs Device-Related Causes
steps.instructions: Try a known-good breathing circuit and flow sensor assembly. If the issue remains with the Hamilton C1 after known-good external parts are installed, remove the ventilator from service for evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: Hamilton C1 ventilator displayed Alarm: 232007 Check flow sensor tubing during setup/pre-use check. Staff reported concern for unreliable flow sensor readings.
CCR.Cause: Flow sensor tubing issue suspected. Both flow sensor lines inspected for disconnection, reversed connection, kinks, blockage, moisture, contamination, and damage. Known-good flow sensor tubing and flow sensor tested. Persistent alarm after known-good external components indicated possible internal flow sensing or pneumatic measurement fault.
CCR.Resolution: Verified patient safety and removed ventilator from use during troubleshooting. Inspected and corrected flow sensor tubing connections and orientation. Replaced contaminated or damaged tubing/flow sensor as needed. Repeated pre-use check/self-test. Unit returned to service after alarm cleared and testing passed. If alarm persisted, unit was labeled Out of Service and sent for repair evaluation.

title: Hamilton C1 Ventilator - Alarm: 232034 Replace O2 cell
description: Troubleshooting guide for Hamilton C1 Alarm 232034 Replace O2 cell caused by a defective, expired, disconnected, or non-calibrating oxygen cell affecting oxygen monitoring accuracy.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the ventilator from patient use if oxygen monitoring is unreliable. Transfer the patient to another ventilator or approved backup ventilation device before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the display shows Alarm: 232034 Replace O2 cell and note whether it appears at startup, during pre-use check, during O2 calibration, during active ventilation, or intermittently after warm-up.

steps.title: Remove the Ventilator from Clinical Use for Sensor Troubleshooting
steps.instructions: Label the ventilator as not ready for patient use and move it to a safe bench area if possible.

steps.title: Check Basic Power and Startup Condition
steps.instructions: Connect the ventilator to AC power, allow normal startup, and confirm whether the alarm clears or remains present.

steps.title: Inspect the O2 Cell Installation
steps.instructions: Check that the O2 cell is present, seated, connected, not damaged, not expired, and not contaminated by moisture.

steps.title: Replace the O2 Cell
steps.instructions: Install the correct approved replacement oxygen cell and confirm the replacement is new, compatible, and not expired.

steps.title: Allow Required Stabilization Time
steps.instructions: Allow the replacement O2 cell to stabilize or warm up as required before attempting calibration.

steps.title: Perform O2 Calibration
steps.instructions: Run O2 calibration or the required pre-use check and verify that calibration passes and the alarm clears.

steps.title: Check the Oxygen Source and Supply Path
steps.instructions: Verify the wall oxygen source, O2 hose, inlet connection, cylinder supply if used, oxygen pressure, and externally accessible inlet filter condition.

steps.title: Inspect O2 Sensor Connection and Related External Conditions
steps.instructions: Inspect the sensor connection area for loose connection, bent contacts, moisture, contamination, corrosion, or evidence of fluid intrusion.

steps.title: Repeat Startup, Calibration, and Pre-Use Check
steps.instructions: Power cycle if appropriate, repeat O2 calibration, run the required pre-use check, and confirm the alarm does not return.

CCR.Complaint: Hamilton C1 ventilator displayed Alarm 232034 Replace O2 cell during startup and failed O2 calibration.
CCR.Cause: Oxygen cell was defective, expired, disconnected, or no longer able to calibrate. Oxygen source and external setup were checked before replacement.
CCR.Resolution: Removed ventilator from service, replaced O2 cell, allowed stabilization time, performed O2 calibration, completed pre-use check, verified alarm cleared, and returned ventilator to service.

title: Hamilton C1 Ventilator - Alarm: 232038 CO2 calibration needed
description: Troubleshooting guide for Hamilton C1 Alarm 232038 CO2 calibration needed when CO2 monitoring requires calibration before readings can be considered reliable.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot or calibrate the CO2 sensor on an active patient if reliable ventilation or monitoring could be affected. Remove the ventilator from patient use if the alarm affects clinical monitoring, alarm reliability, or staff confidence. Transfer the patient to another ventilator or approved backup monitoring method before troubleshooting if needed.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the ventilator displays Alarm: 232038 CO2 calibration needed. Observe when the alarm appears and whether CO2 values are missing, unstable, incorrect, or unavailable.

steps.title: Confirm the CO2 Sensor Is Connected Properly
steps.instructions: Inspect the CO2 sensor connection, cable, pins, and seating. Reconnect the sensor securely and confirm the ventilator recognizes it consistently.

steps.title: Inspect the Airway Adapter
steps.instructions: Check the airway adapter for cracks, moisture, secretions, contamination, incorrect type, poor sensor fit, or obstruction in the optical path. Replace the adapter if it is dirty, wet, damaged, or questionable.

steps.title: Remove the Sensor From Patient or Exhaled CO2 Before Calibration
steps.instructions: Perform calibration away from the patient circuit, exhaled gas, and any CO2 source using room air conditions required by the approved procedure.

steps.title: Perform CO2 Calibration
steps.instructions: Perform CO2 calibration according to the approved Hamilton C1 procedure. Allow calibration to complete without disturbing the sensor, cable, or adapter. Confirm the alarm clears and CO2 readings are stable.

steps.title: Repeat With a Known-Good CO2 Sensor or Adapter
steps.instructions: If calibration fails or the alarm returns, test with a known-good airway adapter and CO2 sensor if available. Swap one item at a time when possible.

steps.title: Check for Environmental or Setup Issues
steps.instructions: Confirm calibration was not attempted near exhaled gas, moisture, cleaning residue, nebulized medication, secretions, or other contamination. Allow the sensor to dry fully if recently cleaned.

steps.title: Confirm Alarm Resolution
steps.instructions: Reconnect the CO2 sensor and airway adapter, verify Alarm 232038 is cleared, confirm CO2 values display normally, and complete any required pre-use check or facility verification.

CCR.Complaint: Hamilton C1 ventilator displayed Alarm 232038 CO2 calibration needed. Staff reported CO2 monitoring was unavailable or not considered reliable.
CCR.Cause: CO2 sensor/system required calibration. CO2 sensor connection and airway adapter were inspected. Calibration was performed away from patient circuit and exhaled CO2 source.
CCR.Resolution: Performed CO2 calibration per procedure. Verified CO2 sensor connection and airway adapter condition. Alarm cleared, CO2 monitoring displayed normally, and ventilator was returned to service.

title: Hamilton C1 Ventilator - Alarm: 231027 Wrong expiratory valve
description: Troubleshooting Alarm: 231027 Wrong expiratory valve on the Hamilton C1 ventilator when the device detects an incorrect, mismatched, damaged, contaminated, or improperly seated expiratory valve setup that may affect circuit readiness and pre-use testing.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot the expiratory valve setup while the ventilator is actively supporting a patient unless immediate clinical safety is already maintained. Remove the ventilator from patient use if the alarm affects ventilation, startup checks, pre-use testing, or circuit readiness. Provide alternate ventilation support if needed.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact displayed alarm is Alarm: 231027 Wrong expiratory valve. Observe when the alarm appears, such as during startup, pre-use check, after circuit changes, after cleaning, or after switching adult/neonatal configuration.

steps.title: Check Adult vs Neonatal Configuration
steps.instructions: Confirm the ventilator is set up for the intended patient type. Verify that the circuit, expiratory valve assembly, and valve cover match the selected adult/pediatric or neonatal configuration.

steps.title: Inspect the Expiratory Valve Assembly
steps.instructions: Remove the expiratory valve assembly according to normal user-accessible handling. Inspect for the wrong valve, incorrect cover, missing components, damaged parts, contamination, fluid, debris, damaged seals, or improper membrane placement.

steps.title: Verify Proper Valve Seating
steps.instructions: Remove and reseat the expiratory valve assembly. Make sure it sits flush, locks into place, and is not cocked, loose, partially engaged, or visibly misaligned.

steps.title: Check for Cleaning or Reprocessing Mix-Ups
steps.instructions: Ask whether the alarm started after cleaning, reassembly, storage, or reprocessing. Check for swapped components between ventilators or between adult and neonatal setups.

steps.title: Install a Known-Good Correct Expiratory Valve Assembly
steps.instructions: Install a known-good expiratory valve assembly that matches the intended Hamilton C1 configuration. If the alarm clears, the original valve assembly, cover, or related component was likely incorrect, damaged, contaminated, or mismatched.

steps.title: Check the Breathing Circuit Setup
steps.instructions: Inspect the patient circuit and confirm the correct circuit type, flow sensor setup, humidifier or HME configuration, and connection orientation. Make sure the circuit setup matches the selected patient category.

steps.title: Rerun the Pre-Use Check
steps.instructions: After correcting the valve and circuit setup, rerun the required pre-use check or system test. Confirm the wrong expiratory valve alarm clears and the ventilator reaches ready-for-use status.

steps.title: Verify Final Operation Before Return to Service
steps.instructions: Confirm the ventilator powers on normally and does not re-alarm after setup. Return the ventilator to service only if the alarm is cleared and testing passes according to facility policy.

CCR.Complaint: Hamilton C1 ventilator displayed Alarm: 231027 Wrong expiratory valve during setup/pre-use check and would not clear for patient use after expiratory valve installation.
CCR.Cause: Installed expiratory valve/cover did not match the expected configuration, was not fully seated, or the ventilator continued to detect a mismatched expiratory valve after setup verification.
CCR.Resolution: Removed ventilator from patient use, verified adult/neonatal configuration, inspected and reseated expiratory valve assembly and cover, checked circuit setup, installed known-good correct expiratory valve assembly, and reran pre-use check. Device passed testing and was returned to service, or remained out of service for repair due to persistent alarm.

title: Hamilton C1 Ventilator - Alarm: 232004 Pressure not released
description: Troubleshooting guide for Hamilton C1 Alarm 232004 Pressure not released, including patient safety steps, external circuit checks, expiratory valve inspection, sensing tubing checks, and escalation when pressure release behavior cannot be verified.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not continue using the ventilator on a patient if pressure is not being released normally. Remove the ventilator from patient use if the alarm is active, recurring, or associated with abnormal pressure behavior. Transfer the patient to another ventilator or approved manual ventilation method as clinically appropriate.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the ventilator display shows Alarm: 232004 Pressure not released. Observe whether the alarm occurs during startup, pre-use check, ventilation, after disconnecting the circuit, after exhalation, or intermittently during use.

steps.title: Check the Patient Circuit for Obstruction
steps.instructions: Inspect the inspiratory and expiratory limbs for kinks, crushed tubing, blocked tubing, water accumulation, incorrect routing, foreign material, or contamination. Straighten or replace any questionable circuit tubing.

steps.title: Check Filters, HMEs, and Inline Accessories
steps.instructions: Inspect the expiratory filter, inspiratory filter, HME, nebulizer adapter, CO2 airway adapter, bacterial/viral filter, and inline accessories. Replace any wet, clogged, contaminated, or restricted component.

steps.title: Inspect the Expiratory Valve Assembly
steps.instructions: Remove and inspect the expiratory valve assembly for incorrect installation, loose seating, damaged membrane, moisture, contamination, sticky movement, cracked housing, or wrong configuration. Reinstall the correct valve and confirm it is fully seated.

steps.title: Check for Incorrect Circuit or Valve Configuration
steps.instructions: Verify adult/pediatric versus neonatal setup, correct expiratory valve type, correct flow sensor setup, correct breathing circuit type, and correct humidifier or HME configuration. Make sure incompatible components have not been mixed.

steps.title: Check Flow Sensor and Pressure-Sensing Tubing
steps.instructions: Inspect flow sensor tubing and pressure-related tubing for moisture, kinks, disconnection, incorrect connection, blocked lines, reversed tubing, or contamination. Replace questionable tubing or flow sensor components as needed.

steps.title: Power Cycle and Repeat Pre-Use Check
steps.instructions: Keep the ventilator disconnected from patient use. Power cycle the ventilator if appropriate, install known-good external components, and run the required pre-use check or system test.

steps.title: Isolate Accessory-Related Versus Device-Related Failure
steps.instructions: Test with known-good circuit, expiratory valve assembly, filters, flow sensor tubing, and correct configuration settings. If the alarm remains with known-good external parts, suspect an internal pneumatic release, pressure sensing, valve control, or manifold issue.

CCR.Complaint: Hamilton C1 ventilator reported Alarm: 232004 Pressure not released. Staff stated pressure appeared to remain in the pneumatic system during use or pre-use check and the ventilator was removed from patient care.
CCR.Cause: Patient circuit, filters, expiratory valve assembly, and flow sensor tubing were inspected. Alarm indicated pressure was not venting as expected. External restrictions were checked and persistent alarm suggested possible internal pneumatic pressure release or valve control failure.
CCR.Resolution: Removed ventilator from service. Checked circuit for obstruction, inspected filters and inline accessories, verified expiratory valve seating, checked flow sensor tubing, and attempted pre-use check with known-good external components. Alarm persisted, so device was labeled Out of Service and sent for repair/bench evaluation.

title: Hamilton C1 Ventilator - Alarm: 231017 Blower service required
description: Troubleshooting guide for Hamilton C1 ventilator Alarm: 231017 Blower service required, including patient safety, external airflow checks, filter inspection, cooling checks, pre-use testing, and escalation for blower-related service evaluation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the Hamilton C1 from patient use if ventilation could be affected. Place the patient on an alternate ventilator or approved manual ventilation support before troubleshooting the blower service alarm.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the ventilator displays Alarm: 231017 Blower service required. Document whether it occurs during startup, pre-use check, ventilation, after warm-up, or under high-demand settings.

steps.title: Remove the Device From Clinical Use Until Evaluated
steps.instructions: Do not return the ventilator to patient care simply because the alarm clears after restart. Label the device Out of Service if the alarm is reproducible or unexplained.

steps.title: Check Power Source and Startup Behavior
steps.instructions: Connect the ventilator to a known-good AC outlet, inspect the power cord and plug, and observe startup behavior for instability or repeated alarm recurrence.

steps.title: Check the Air Inlet Path
steps.instructions: Inspect the air intake area for blockage, dust, lint, debris, restricted clearance, or placement against walls, carts, blankets, or other equipment.

steps.title: Inspect External Filters
steps.instructions: Check the air inlet filter for dirt, clogging, moisture, discoloration, damage, incorrect installation, or overdue replacement. Replace only with the correct Hamilton C1 filter type.

steps.title: Check Cooling Airflow Around the Device
steps.instructions: Inspect cooling vents, confirm the ventilator is not covered or confined, check for nearby heat sources, and listen for abnormal fan or blower noise.

steps.title: Review Service History and Blower Operating Hours
steps.instructions: Review preventive maintenance records, prior blower or airflow-related repairs, and blower-related service counters if available through approved service access.

steps.title: Run Approved Pre-Use Check or Self-Test
steps.instructions: Use a known-good patient circuit and required test lung if appropriate. Perform the approved pre-use check or system test and document any failure or alarm recurrence.

steps.title: Isolate External Versus Device-Related Causes
steps.instructions: Confirm AC power, inlet airflow, filters, cooling airflow, patient circuit, service history, and setup have been checked. If the alarm persists, suspect an internal blower service condition or related device fault.

CCR.Complaint: Hamilton C1 ventilator displayed Alarm: 231017 Blower service required during startup/pre-use check. Staff removed device from patient use due to possible blower service condition.
CCR.Cause: External checks completed. AC power, air inlet path, filter condition, cooling airflow, patient circuit, and service history were reviewed. Alarm persisted, indicating likely internal blower service threshold or blower-related device fault.
CCR.Resolution: Removed ventilator from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for service-level evaluation/repair. Documented Alarm: 231017 Blower service required, alarm timing, pre-use check result, and completed external troubleshooting checks.

title: Hamilton C1 Ventilator - Alarm: 246003 Fan failure
description: Troubleshooting Alarm: 246003 Fan failure on a Hamilton C1 ventilator when cooling airflow may be blocked, the fan is not operating correctly, or overheating risk exists.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot a cooling fan failure while the ventilator is supporting a patient unless clinical staff confirm it is safe. Remove the Hamilton C1 from patient use if the alarm is active, the ventilator feels hot, the fan is not running, ventilation performance is affected, or the alarm repeats after restart. Transfer the patient to another ventilator or approved backup ventilation device.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the display shows Alarm: 246003 Fan failure. Observe whether the alarm occurs at startup, after warm-up, during high workload ventilation, intermittently, or after cleaning, transport, or storage. Check for no fan operation, weak airflow, abnormal fan noise, excessive heat, dust buildup, or other technical alarms.

steps.title: Remove the Ventilator From Bedside Obstructions
steps.instructions: Inspect the ventilator location and make sure cooling vents are not blocked by bedding, drapes, papers, equipment covers, wall contact, carts, accessory bags, cleaning wipes, or packaging. Move the ventilator to an open area with proper ventilation clearance and verify the alarm does not return.

steps.title: Inspect Vents for Dust or Contamination
steps.instructions: Check cooling intake and exhaust areas for dust, lint, debris, fluid residue, tape, labels, or cleaning chemical buildup. Clean only external vent areas according to hospital-approved cleaning practice and do not force tools deep into the device.

steps.title: Listen and Feel for Fan Operation
steps.instructions: Power the ventilator in a safe bench area or non-patient setting. Check for normal fan sound, air movement at the vent, abnormal grinding or clicking noise, fan cycling briefly and stopping, or no fan operation. Do not return the ventilator to service if the fan is not running or sounds abnormal.

steps.title: Check for External Signs of Overheating
steps.instructions: Inspect for a warm or hot housing, heat smell, temperature-related alarms, shutdown or restart behavior, or fan alarm appearing after the unit warms up. If overheating is suspected, power down the device, label it Out of Service, and send it for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Check Recent Service, Cleaning, or Transport History
steps.instructions: Ask staff or review service history for recent cleaning, filter or accessory work, drops, impacts, dusty storage, transport, prior intermittent fan alarms, or previous overheating complaints. Use this information to identify likely external triggers or recurring failure patterns.

steps.title: Inspect Accessible Fan Wiring Only if Appropriate
