steps.instructions: Perform only non-invasive or approved inspection allowed by facility procedure. If safe and authorized, check for obvious fan connection issues such as a loose visible connector, pinched wiring, damaged harness, fluid intrusion, or obvious fan obstruction. Do not perform board-level repair or deep internal disassembly unless trained and authorized.

steps.title: Isolate the Likely Failure
steps.instructions: Classify the issue as blocked vent, dust buildup, fan obstruction, fan failure, wiring issue, or internal device fault. If the alarm persists despite normal external conditions, suspect internal fan failure, wiring failure, fan control failure, or cooling system fault.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: Run the ventilator in a safe test setup. Confirm Alarm: 246003 Fan failure does not return, the fan runs normally, no overheating occurs, no related technical alarms appear, and required pre-use or operational checks pass before returning the ventilator to service.

CCR.Complaint: Hamilton C1 ventilator reported Alarm: 246003 Fan failure. Staff stated the cooling fan alarm appeared during operation, and the unit was removed from patient use for evaluation.
CCR.Cause: External vents were inspected for blockage and dust buildup. Fan operation was checked. Alarm persisted after external airflow checks, indicating likely internal cooling fan, fan wiring, or fan control failure.
CCR.Resolution: Removed ventilator from service and labeled Out of Service. Sent for repair/bench evaluation due to persistent fan failure alarm and potential overheating risk. Device not returned to clinical use pending repair and operational verification.

title: Hamilton C1 Ventilator - Alarm: 232027 Device temperature high
description: Troubleshooting Alarm 232027 Device temperature high on a Hamilton C1 ventilator when internal device temperature is elevated and safe ventilator operation may be affected.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the Hamilton C1 from patient use if the alarm is active, recurring, or associated with unusual heat, smell, fan noise, shutdown behavior, or performance issues. Transfer the patient to another ventilator or approved backup ventilation method before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the display shows Alarm: 232027 Device temperature high. Note when the alarm occurs and check for unusual heat, abnormal fan behavior, blocked vents, reduced airflow, shutdowns, restarts, or other technical alarms.

steps.title: Remove the Ventilator From Heat Stress
steps.instructions: Move the ventilator away from direct sunlight, radiant warmers, heat-producing equipment, blankets, drapes, or crowded equipment carts. Confirm the room temperature is reasonable and the ventilator has open airflow around the housing.

steps.title: Check for Blocked Vents or Restricted Airflow
steps.instructions: Inspect all external vents and air intake areas for blocked openings, dust buildup, lint, tape, labels, plastic covers, linens, drapes, or equipment mounted too close to vent openings. Clear external obstructions only.

steps.title: Inspect for Dust Buildup Around Air Intake Areas
steps.instructions: Check visible intake and exhaust areas for heavy dust or lint. If facility procedure allows, clean external vent surfaces using approved methods. Do not force debris deeper into the ventilator and do not use liquid near vent openings.

steps.title: Check Cooling Fan Operation
steps.instructions: Listen for fan operation during startup and while the ventilator is running. If the fan is not running, sounds abnormal, runs intermittently, or the alarm returns after the unit warms up, remove the ventilator from service.

steps.title: Allow the Ventilator to Cool
steps.instructions: If the alarm followed heat exposure or extended operation, power the device down if clinically safe and allowed by procedure, place it in a well-ventilated room-temperature area, allow it to cool, then restart and observe for recurrence.

steps.title: Check Power and External Setup
steps.instructions: Confirm the ventilator is connected to an appropriate power source. Inspect the power cord and external power connection for damage, looseness, or heat. Verify mounting hardware, cable routing, and nearby surfaces are not restricting airflow.

steps.title: Run a Basic Operational Check After Cooling
steps.instructions: Power on the ventilator after correcting external causes. Confirm the alarm does not immediately return, allow the device to run long enough to verify stable operation, and perform required facility pre-use or operational checks before clinical use.

steps.title: Isolate the Likely Cause
steps.instructions: If the alarm clears after removing heat sources or airflow restrictions, classify it as environmental or airflow-related. If the fan is noisy, not running, or the alarm returns after cooling, suspect an internal cooling, fan, sensor, or control issue.

CCR.Complaint: Hamilton C1 ventilator reported Alarm 232027 Device temperature high. Staff stated the ventilator became warm during use and the alarm returned after running.
CCR.Cause: Checked ambient temperature, nearby heat sources, vent openings, dust buildup, air intake path, power connection, and external obstructions. External airflow was acceptable. Cooling fan operation was abnormal and the alarm returned after cooldown, indicating likely internal cooling system failure.
CCR.Resolution: Removed ventilator from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for bench evaluation/repair. Device was not returned to patient use due to recurring high internal temperature alarm.

title: Hamilton C1 Ventilator - Alarm: 231011 Vent outlet temperature high
description: Troubleshooting guide for Hamilton C1 Alarm: 231011 Vent outlet temperature high when elevated outlet temperature may affect safe gas delivery, cooling performance, and ventilator reliability.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the ventilator from patient use if the alarm is active, recurring, or associated with abnormal heat, odor, airflow changes, fan issues, or ventilation instability. Transfer the patient to another approved ventilator or backup ventilation method before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the displayed alarm is Alarm: 231011 Vent outlet temperature high. Note when the alarm occurs and check for related symptoms such as warm outlet gas path, fan noise changes, blocked vents, reduced airflow, abnormal odor, or other fan, blower, or temperature-related alarms.

steps.title: Check Ambient Room Temperature
steps.instructions: Verify the ventilator is not operating in an excessively warm area. Look for nearby heat sources such as radiant warmers, direct sunlight, heating vents, blankets, covers, or other equipment exhausting heat toward the ventilator.

steps.title: Inspect Air Vents and Cooling Airflow
steps.instructions: Inspect all visible air intake and exhaust vents. Make sure the ventilator is not pushed against a wall, cart panel, bedding, curtain, bag, or other equipment. Remove visible dust, lint, or debris from the external vent area using facility-approved cleaning practices.

steps.title: Check Heated Circuit and Humidifier Setup
steps.instructions: Verify whether the ventilator is connected to a heated wire circuit, active humidifier, humidifier chamber, temperature probe, or circuit heater cable. Confirm the humidifier and heated circuit are assembled and configured correctly.

steps.title: Inspect the Patient Circuit for Obstruction or Flow Restriction
steps.instructions: Inspect the breathing circuit for kinks, blocked filters, occluded tubing, water accumulation, incorrect assembly, saturated filters, or improper expiratory valve setup. Replace any questionable circuit, filter, valve assembly, or accessory.

steps.title: Check Fan Operation Externally
steps.instructions: Listen for normal cooling fan operation. Check for abnormal fan sounds, grinding, intermittent fan operation, or no fan sound when the device is warm. Do not insert tools or objects into fan or vent openings.

steps.title: Allow the Ventilator to Cool
steps.instructions: Remove the ventilator from patient use, power it down if appropriate, move it to a normal room-temperature area with clear airflow, allow it to cool, then restart and observe whether the alarm returns.

steps.title: Retest with a Known-Good External Setup
steps.instructions: Use a known-good power source, known-good patient circuit, correct filters, correct humidifier setup, and clear space around all vents. Run the ventilator long enough to determine whether the alarm returns.

CCR.Complaint: Hamilton C1 ventilator displayed Alarm: 231011 Vent outlet temperature high during operation. Staff reported the outlet/cooling area appeared warmer than expected.
CCR.Cause: External checks performed. Room temperature, vent clearance, circuit setup, filters, humidifier/heated circuit setup, and cooling airflow were inspected. Alarm returned after cooling and retest, indicating likely internal cooling, blower, outlet airflow, or temperature sensing issue.
CCR.Resolution: Ventilator removed from patient use, labeled Out of Service, and sent for repair evaluation. Device not returned to service due to recurring outlet temperature alarm.

title: Hamilton C1 Ventilator - Alarm: 243003 Loudspeaker defective
description: Troubleshooting Alarm: 243003 Loudspeaker defective on a Hamilton C1 ventilator when audible alarm output may be unavailable, weak, distorted, or unsafe for patient monitoring.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the ventilator from patient use if Alarm: 243003 is active, recurring, or associated with weak, distorted, intermittent, or absent audible alarm sound. Move the patient to another ventilator or approved backup ventilation method before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the displayed alarm is Alarm: 243003 Loudspeaker defective. Ask staff when the alarm appeared and document whether audible alarm sound was absent, weak, distorted, intermittent, or normal.

steps.title: Check Alarm Volume and Silence Status
steps.instructions: Verify the alarm volume setting, confirm the ventilator is not silenced or muted, and perform an approved alarm sound check only when the ventilator is off patient use.

steps.title: Inspect the Loudspeaker Area Externally
steps.instructions: Check for tape, labels, debris, cleaning residue, fluid contamination, dust buildup, cracked housing, or signs of impact damage near the speaker openings. Do not open the ventilator housing during basic troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify Power and Startup Conditions
steps.instructions: Confirm AC power, battery status, and startup behavior. Restart the ventilator only after it has been removed from patient use, then observe whether Alarm: 243003 returns.

steps.title: Isolate the Likely Cause
steps.instructions: If external causes are ruled out and the alarm returns, suspect a failed loudspeaker, loose internal speaker connection, alarm audio circuit fault, or control hardware issue. Remove the ventilator from service for repair.

CCR.Complaint: Hamilton C1 ventilator displayed Alarm: 243003 Loudspeaker defective. Staff reported concern that audible alarm output may not be functioning properly.
CCR.Cause: Audible alarm safety fault confirmed. Alarm volume, silence status, speaker obstruction, power condition, and external damage were checked. Alarm persisted, indicating likely loudspeaker or internal alarm circuit failure.
CCR.Resolution: Removed ventilator from patient use, labeled Out of Service, and sent for repair or bench evaluation. Device not returned to service pending verification of audible alarm function.

title: Hamilton C1 Ventilator - Alarm: 243004 Buzzer defective
description: Troubleshooting guide for Hamilton C1 Alarm 243004 Buzzer defective, where the backup audible alarm may fail during startup, self-check, or alarm testing.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot this alarm while the ventilator is supporting a patient. Remove the Hamilton C1 from patient use if Alarm 243004 is active, recurring, or reported. Transfer the patient to another ventilator or approved backup ventilation method before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Power on the ventilator only when it is not connected to a patient. Observe startup and self-check, confirm the exact displayed alarm is Alarm: 243004 Buzzer defective, and note when the alarm appears.

steps.title: Confirm Audible Alarm Function
steps.instructions: Use an approved alarm test or pre-use check process to verify audible alarm output. Confirm whether the main alarm speaker and backup buzzer sound when expected.

steps.title: Check External and Simple Causes First
steps.instructions: Confirm known-good AC power, inspect the power cord and inlet, verify battery condition, and check for fluid intrusion, impact damage, contamination, unusual heat, or blocked vents.

steps.title: Check Alarm Volume and Configuration
steps.instructions: Verify alarm volume is not turned down, confirm normal alarm tones are audible from the main speaker, and confirm staff are reporting the Buzzer defective alarm rather than only a quiet alarm.

steps.title: Repeat Startup or Pre-Use Check
steps.instructions: Power cycle the ventilator only when it is not connected to a patient. Allow startup or pre-use check to complete and observe whether Alarm 243004 returns.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Issue Is Device-Related
steps.instructions: If external checks are acceptable and the alarm persists, suspect a failed backup buzzer, disconnected buzzer wiring, alarm driver circuit fault, internal alarm hardware failure, or startup self-check detection failure.

CCR.Complaint: Hamilton C1 ventilator reported Alarm 243004 Buzzer defective during startup/self-check. Backup audible alarm function may not be working.
CCR.Cause: Backup buzzer failed startup/self-check. External power, alarm volume, battery status, and basic physical condition checked. Persistent alarm indicates likely internal buzzer or alarm hardware failure.
CCR.Resolution: Removed ventilator from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for bench evaluation/repair of buzzer or alarm circuitry. Device not returned to patient use.

title: Hamilton C1 Ventilator - Alarm: 244001 Loss of external power
description: Troubleshooting guide for Alarm: 244001 Loss of external power on the Hamilton C1 ventilator when AC power is lost and the ventilator may be operating on battery.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient is being ventilated properly, check battery status and remaining runtime, notify clinical staff, and prepare a backup ventilator or alternate ventilation method if external power cannot be restored quickly.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the ventilator displays Alarm: 244001 Loss of external power and determine whether the unit is running on battery, whether the alarm is intermittent, and whether it is associated with a specific outlet, cord movement, transport, or facility power issue.

steps.title: Check the AC Power Cord
steps.instructions: Confirm the AC power cord is fully seated at the ventilator or external power supply connection and at the wall outlet. Inspect for damage, loose strain relief, bent prongs, overheating, or intermittent power loss when the cord is lightly moved.

steps.title: Check the Wall Outlet
steps.instructions: Move the ventilator to a known-good hospital-grade outlet, avoid questionable power strips or extension cords, test the original outlet if appropriate, and notify Facilities if the issue follows the outlet.

steps.title: Check the External Power Supply or Adapter
steps.instructions: Verify the correct power supply is being used, confirm all inline connections are seated, inspect the adapter for damage or overheating, and test with an approved spare power supply if available.

steps.title: Check for Movement-Related Power Loss
steps.instructions: Ask whether the alarm started after transport, repositioning, or switching outlets. Inspect cord routing for tension, pulling, or pinching from the ventilator stand, bed movement, or cable management.

steps.title: Check Battery Status
steps.instructions: Check battery charge level, battery-related messages, and whether the battery begins charging after external power is restored. Remove the ventilator from service if battery status is poor or charging does not resume.

steps.title: Check Facility Power Conditions
steps.instructions: Ask whether the room, outlet bank, transport cart, or area recently lost power. Confirm emergency power availability if required and document any facility-side power issue.

steps.title: Confirm Alarm Clears and External Power Is Stable
steps.instructions: Reconnect the ventilator to a known-good power source, confirm the alarm clears, verify the external power or charging indicator behaves normally, and monitor the ventilator long enough to confirm the alarm does not return.

CCR.Complaint: Hamilton C1 ventilator reported Alarm: 244001 Loss of external power. Staff stated ventilator was running on battery while connected to wall power.
CCR.Cause: AC power cord was loose at the external power connection. Wall outlet tested good, and battery status was acceptable after external power was restored.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated AC power cord, verified external power indicator returned, confirmed battery charging, and monitored ventilator with no recurrence of alarm. Device returned to service.

title: Hamilton C1 Ventilator - Alarm: 244006 Battery low
description: Troubleshooting guide for Hamilton C1 Alarm: 244006 Battery low when battery runtime is limited, charging may be abnormal, or the ventilator may lose power without external power.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Treat the low battery alarm as a patient safety issue. Connect external AC power immediately if the ventilator is in active use, confirm ventilation remains stable, and remove the device from patient use if power stability cannot be confirmed.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the display shows Alarm: 244006 Battery low. Note whether it occurs during transport, after unplugging from AC power, at startup, after storage, while connected to AC power, or during movement.

steps.title: Connect External Power Immediately
steps.instructions: Plug the ventilator into a known working AC outlet, verify the power cord is fully seated, confirm the external power indicator is active, and allow the battery to begin charging.

steps.title: Verify the Wall Outlet and Power Source
steps.instructions: Test the outlet, check for switched outlets or questionable power strips, and move the ventilator to a known good outlet if needed.

steps.title: Inspect the Power Cord and Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect the AC power cord for damage, loose strain relief, bent pins, heat damage, or intermittent connection. Reseat the cord and try a known good compatible cord if available.

steps.title: Check Battery Charging Status
steps.instructions: With AC power connected, observe the battery charge indicator, allow charging time, confirm the charge level increases, and check whether the alarm clears.

steps.title: Check Battery Seating and External Condition
steps.instructions: Remove the ventilator from clinical use before handling the battery. Confirm the battery is seated and latched, and inspect for swelling, contamination, corrosion, loose contacts, or damage.

steps.title: Check Battery Age and Runtime History
steps.instructions: Review battery age, service history, and runtime complaints. Check whether the battery drops quickly after unplugging or fails to support expected transport use.

steps.title: Replace the Battery if It Will Not Hold Charge
steps.instructions: Install a known good compatible battery if available, charge the ventilator, confirm the alarm clears, and verify acceptable battery runtime during bench testing.

steps.title: Isolate Charging or Internal Power Issues
steps.instructions: If the alarm remains with verified AC power, a known good cord, proper battery seating, and a known good battery, suspect an internal charging circuit, battery interface, power supply, or monitoring issue.

CCR.Complaint: Hamilton C1 ventilator reported Alarm: 244006 Battery low. Staff stated battery runtime was limited and the ventilator alarmed after being unplugged from AC power.
CCR.Cause: Battery charge was low. External AC power, outlet, and power cord were checked. Battery charging status and seating were inspected. Battery condition suggested reduced capacity and unreliable runtime.
CCR.Resolution: Connected ventilator to verified AC power and confirmed charging status. Battery was reseated and evaluated. Battery was replaced after failing to hold adequate charge. Alarm cleared after charging with replacement battery. Ventilator passed operational checks and was returned to service.

title: Hamilton C1 Ventilator - Alarm: 244011 Battery communication error
description: Troubleshooting guide for Hamilton C1 Alarm 244011 Battery communication error, where the ventilator cannot communicate correctly with the battery system and battery status or backup power reliability may be unreliable.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the ventilator from patient use if it is actively alarming, running on battery, or if battery status cannot be trusted. Connect to reliable external AC power and transfer the patient to another ventilator if ventilation or backup power reliability is uncertain.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the screen displays Alarm: 244011 Battery communication error. Check battery icon behavior, charge indication, charging status, external power status, and any additional battery or power alarms.

steps.title: Connect to External Power
steps.instructions: Plug the ventilator into a known-good AC outlet, confirm the AC power indicator is present, verify the power cord is fully seated, and check whether battery charging or battery information returns to normal.

steps.title: Inspect the Battery Installation
steps.instructions: Power down only if safe, remove the battery, inspect whether it was fully inserted and latched, reinstall it firmly, restart the ventilator, and check whether the alarm returns.

steps.title: Inspect Battery Contacts
steps.instructions: Inspect battery and ventilator-side contacts for bent pins, contamination, corrosion, fluid residue, damage, debris, loose contacts, or recessed contacts. Clean only according to approved practices.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good Battery
steps.instructions: Install a known-good compatible Hamilton C1 battery and verify battery recognition, charge status, and alarm status. If the fault follows one battery, suspect the battery. If it remains with known-good batteries, suspect a ventilator-side issue.

steps.title: Check Battery Age and Condition
steps.instructions: Review battery date, service history, and maintenance records. Inspect for swelling, case damage, leakage, overheating, unusual smell, or repeated charge and runtime complaints.

steps.title: Check for Related Power or Charging Issues
steps.instructions: Verify charging behavior, battery percentage changes, external power stability, absence of repeated low battery alarms, and no unexpected shutdowns or reboots.

steps.title: Isolate the Likely Cause
steps.instructions: Use findings to determine whether the issue is poor seating, contact-related, a defective battery, intermittent latch/contact trouble, or a ventilator-side battery manager or communication hardware fault.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: Confirm the ventilator recognizes the battery, charging status is normal, no power alarms return after restart, external power and battery status are stable, and required operational or pre-use checks pass.

CCR.Complaint: Hamilton C1 ventilator reported Alarm 244011 Battery communication error. Staff stated the ventilator displayed a battery communication fault and battery status could not be confirmed.
CCR.Cause: Battery communication between the ventilator and battery system was abnormal. Battery was reseated, contacts were inspected, and known-good battery testing was performed to determine whether the issue followed the battery or remained with the ventilator.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated battery, inspected battery contacts, tested unit on external AC power, and verified battery status. Installed known-good battery for comparison. Ventilator passed power and battery checks and was returned to service, or was removed from service for battery manager/internal communication repair evaluation if the alarm persisted.

title: Hamilton C1 Ventilator - Alarm: 244012 Battery 1: Defective
description: Troubleshooting guide for Hamilton C1 ventilator Alarm 244012 Battery 1: Defective when Battery 1 has failed, is not charging, or is not recognized safely.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot a ventilator battery failure while relying on that battery for active patient ventilation. Transfer the patient to a backup ventilator or confirm continuous AC power and clinical backup before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the screen displays Alarm 244012 Battery 1: Defective. Observe when the alarm appears and whether Battery 1 is not recognized, not charging, showing permanent failure, or showing abnormal runtime.

steps.title: Confirm External Power Is Connected
steps.instructions: Connect the ventilator to a known-good AC outlet. Verify the power cord is fully seated and confirm the external power indicator shows the ventilator is receiving AC power.

steps.title: Inspect Battery 1 Seating
steps.instructions: Remove and reinstall Battery 1 only when safe and appropriate. Confirm the battery is fully seated, latched, and installed in the correct battery position.

steps.title: Inspect Battery Contacts
steps.instructions: Inspect the battery and battery bay contacts for bent pins, corrosion, debris, fluid contamination, burn marks, physical damage, or recessed contacts. Do not scrape, bend, modify, or force contacts.

steps.title: Replace Battery 1 With an Approved Compatible Battery
steps.instructions: Install a known-good approved Hamilton-compatible battery in the Battery 1 position. Confirm the battery locks into place, is recognized, and begins charging normally.

steps.title: Confirm Charging and Battery Recognition
steps.instructions: With AC power connected, verify Battery 1 is detected, charging status appears normal, no defective alarm returns, and no battery communication alarm appears.

steps.title: Check for Battery Bay or Device-Side Issue
steps.instructions: If allowed by facility procedure, compare the suspected battery with a known-good battery. If the alarm follows the battery, suspect battery failure. If the alarm stays with the device, suspect the battery bay, contacts, charging circuit, or battery management hardware.

CCR.Complaint: Hamilton C1 ventilator reported Alarm 244012 Battery 1: Defective. Battery 1 showed defective status and was not considered reliable for backup power.
CCR.Cause: Battery 1 indicated permanent battery failure. External power was verified, battery seating and contacts were inspected, and the fault was isolated to the installed Battery 1.
CCR.Resolution: Removed defective Battery 1 from service and installed an approved compatible replacement battery. Confirmed Battery 1 was recognized, charging normally, and no battery defective alarm returned. Ventilator returned to service after successful checks.

title: Hamilton C1 Ventilator - Alarm: 244013 Battery 2: Defective
description: Troubleshooting guide for Alarm: 244013 Battery 2: Defective on the Hamilton C1 ventilator, where Battery 2 has failed or cannot be trusted for backup power.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot an unresolved ventilator battery fault while the device is supporting a patient unless patient safety is already secured. Transfer the patient to another approved ventilator if clinically appropriate and keep the Hamilton C1 connected to external AC power while evaluating the alarm.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the ventilator displays Alarm: 244013 Battery 2: Defective. Check whether the alarm appears at startup, during battery check, while charging, after unplugging AC power, or intermittently during use.

steps.title: Confirm External Power Is Stable
steps.instructions: Connect the ventilator to a known-good AC outlet. Confirm external power is recognized and inspect the AC power cord, power supply connection, adapter, and wall outlet for looseness or damage.

steps.title: Check Battery 2 Seating
steps.instructions: Power down the ventilator if clinically safe and allowed by facility process. Remove and reseat Battery 2, confirm it is fully latched, restart the ventilator, and check whether the alarm clears.

steps.title: Inspect Battery 2 and Battery Contacts
steps.instructions: Inspect Battery 2 for cracks, swelling, leaking, burn marks, corrosion, contamination, or damaged contacts. Inspect the ventilator battery bay contacts for damage or debris. Do not reinstall a physically damaged battery.

steps.title: Replace Battery 2 With an Approved Compatible Battery
steps.instructions: Install an approved Hamilton-compatible Battery 2 replacement. Confirm the battery is fully seated, power the ventilator on with AC connected, and check whether the Battery 2: Defective alarm clears.

steps.title: Confirm Battery 2 Is Recognized and Charging
steps.instructions: Check the battery status screen or battery indicator. Confirm Battery 2 is detected, charging, showing a reasonable charge level, and not generating additional battery or charging alarms.

steps.title: Compare Battery Slot Behavior if Needed
steps.instructions: If the alarm remains after installing a known-good Battery 2, compare whether the fault follows the battery or stays with the Battery 2 position if facility policy allows. A fault that stays with the slot may indicate ventilator-side battery hardware.

steps.title: Check for Related Power or Battery Alarms
steps.instructions: Look for additional alarms such as Battery communication error, Battery low, Loss of external power, Battery 1 defective, charging failure, or power supply fault. Multiple alarms may indicate a larger power management issue.

CCR.Complaint: Hamilton C1 ventilator displayed Alarm: 244013 Battery 2: Defective. Staff reported Battery 2 warning was present during startup and battery status showed Battery 2 was not usable.
CCR.Cause: Battery 2 had a permanent failure. Battery contacts were inspected and no external damage was found. Alarm cleared after Battery 2 was replaced with an approved compatible battery.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced Battery 2, confirmed replacement battery was recognized and charging normally, verified no active battery alarms remained, and completed operational check. Ventilator returned to service.

title: Hamilton C1 Ventilator - Alarm: 244016 Battery 1: Replacement required
description: Troubleshooting guide for Hamilton C1 Alarm Technical Fault: 244016 Battery 1: Replacement required, where Battery 1 has reached replacement condition and backup runtime may be unreliable.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot or test the ventilator battery system while supporting an active patient. Transfer the patient to another approved ventilator before troubleshooting if battery reliability is in question.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the display shows Alarm: Technical Fault: 244016 Battery 1: Replacement required. Note whether the alarm occurs during startup, self-check, AC operation, battery operation, or after moving the ventilator.

steps.title: Confirm External Power Is Stable
steps.instructions: Verify the ventilator is connected to a working AC outlet. Check the power cord, wall outlet, cord seating, and external power indication.

steps.title: Check Battery 1 Seating
steps.instructions: Remove and reseat Battery 1 only when the ventilator is not in patient use and facility procedure allows it. Confirm the battery is fully inserted, latched, and secure.

steps.title: Inspect Battery 1 Contacts
steps.instructions: Inspect battery and ventilator-side contacts for debris, corrosion, liquid contamination, bent contacts, burn marks, or physical damage. Do not force or modify contacts.

steps.title: Verify Correct Battery Type
steps.instructions: Confirm Battery 1 is an approved Hamilton-compatible battery for the C1 ventilator and is not an incorrect, expired, damaged, or non-approved substitute.

steps.title: Replace Battery 1
steps.instructions: Replace Battery 1 with an approved compatible battery. Confirm the replacement battery is recognized and the alarm clears.

steps.title: Confirm Battery Charging Status
steps.instructions: Leave the ventilator connected to AC power and verify Battery 1 shows normal charging or charge status. Monitor for the alarm returning.

steps.title: Check Runtime If Required
steps.instructions: Perform a battery runtime or operational check only if required by facility procedure. Do not perform extended battery testing on an active patient.

CCR.Complaint: Hamilton C1 ventilator displayed Alarm: Technical Fault: 244016 Battery 1: Replacement required during startup while connected to AC power.
CCR.Cause: Battery 1 had reached replacement condition. External power, battery seating, battery type, and visible contacts were checked with no external power issue found.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced Battery 1 with an approved compatible battery. Verified proper seating, charging status, alarm cleared, and required operational checks passed. Device returned to service.

title: Hamilton C1 Ventilator - Alarm: 244017 Battery 2: Replacement required
description: Troubleshooting Alarm 244017 Battery 2: Replacement required on a Hamilton C1 ventilator when Battery 2 has reached replacement condition and may not provide reliable backup power.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the ventilator from patient use if battery backup reliability is required. Transfer the patient to another approved ventilator before troubleshooting and confirm the Hamilton C1 is connected to reliable external power.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the display shows Alarm 244017 Battery 2: Replacement required. Note when the alarm appears and whether Battery 1 or other power alarms are also present.

steps.title: Confirm External Power Is Stable
steps.instructions: Check the AC power cord, wall outlet, power adapter, and external power indication. Correct any external power issue before evaluating the battery.

steps.title: Check Battery 2 Seating
steps.instructions: Reseat Battery 2 if allowed by facility procedure. Inspect the battery compartment for debris, broken latch points, bent contacts, corrosion, or visible damage.

steps.title: Verify Correct Battery Type
steps.instructions: Confirm Battery 2 is an approved Hamilton-compatible battery for the C1 ventilator and is installed in the correct position.

steps.title: Check Battery Charging Status
steps.instructions: Connect the ventilator to AC power and check whether Battery 2 charges normally, is recognized, or continues to report replacement required.

steps.title: Install a Known-Good Battery if Available
steps.instructions: Replace Battery 2 with a known-good approved battery. Confirm recognition, charging behavior, and alarm clearance.

steps.title: Perform Battery Verification if Required
steps.instructions: Fully charge the replacement battery and perform any facility-required battery check or runtime verification before returning the ventilator to service.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Issue Is Battery-Related or Device-Related
steps.instructions: If the alarm clears with a replacement battery, document the original battery as failed or due for replacement. If the alarm remains with a known-good battery, suspect battery bay, charging, communication, or internal power management issues.

steps.title: Return to Service Only After the Alarm Clears
steps.instructions: Confirm Alarm 244017 is cleared, Battery 2 is recognized and charging or charged, no related battery alarms remain, and required operational checks are complete.

CCR.Complaint: Hamilton C1 ventilator reported Alarm 244017 Battery 2: Replacement required during startup. Staff reported concern that backup battery power may not be reliable.
CCR.Cause: Battery 2 had reached replacement condition. External power was verified, battery bay was inspected, and the installed Battery 2 did not clear the replacement alarm.
CCR.Resolution: Removed ventilator from patient use. Replaced Battery 2 with an approved compatible battery, confirmed battery recognition and charging status, verified Alarm 244017 cleared, and completed required operational checks before returning device to service.

title: Hamilton C1 Ventilator - Alarm: 246001 Preventive maintenance required
description: Troubleshooting Alarm 246001 Preventive maintenance required on a Hamilton C1 ventilator when scheduled maintenance is due and reliability, battery readiness, blower status, filter condition, or service compliance may be affected.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not perform preventive maintenance checks on a ventilator that is actively supporting a patient. Remove the ventilator from patient use if the alarm appears during clinical operation and another ventilator is available. Transfer the patient to another approved ventilator if continued ventilation support is required. Do not reset or clear maintenance status just to silence the alarm.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the ventilator displays Alarm: 246001 Preventive maintenance required. Observe whether the alarm appears during startup, pre-use check, normal operation, after service interval expiration, or continuously. Check for associated alarms, failed checks, battery warnings, filter warnings, blower-related messages, reduced runtime, abnormal fan noise, overheating, unusual sounds, or unusual smells.

steps.title: Check Whether the Ventilator Is Due for Scheduled PM
steps.instructions: Review the equipment maintenance record, CMMS history, asset tag, and last PM completion date. Confirm whether the device is past its scheduled preventive maintenance interval. Check whether the alarm appeared because the PM interval expired, blower service is due, filters are due, battery checks are due, or a prior PM was completed but not properly documented or reset.

steps.title: Inspect External Condition Before Service
steps.instructions: Inspect the ventilator for physical damage, cracked housing, loose connectors, blocked vents, dust buildup, contamination, missing covers, damaged power cord, damaged oxygen inlet or gas fittings, and damaged patient circuit connections. Address visible damage or airflow restriction before returning the ventilator to use.

steps.title: Check Power Source and Charging Status
steps.instructions: Connect the ventilator to a known-good AC outlet. Confirm external power is recognized. Inspect the AC power cord, power adapter, and strain relief. Check battery charging indicators and review battery condition if available.

steps.title: Check Battery Condition
steps.instructions: Confirm the batteries are recognized. Look for battery-related alarms or technical faults. Inspect battery seating and contacts if accessible without deep disassembly. Confirm battery age and replacement history. Perform runtime testing only if required by facility procedure.

steps.title: Check Filters, Airflow, and Cooling Path
steps.instructions: Check external filters according to facility procedure. Inspect air intake and exhaust areas for blockage. Look for dust buildup around vents. Confirm the ventilator is not near a heat source or in a restricted airflow area. Listen for abnormal fan or blower noise.

steps.title: Check Blower and Service Status
steps.instructions: Review whether blower-related service is due. Check the maintenance interval and any available service counters. Look for additional blower-related messages or technical events. Listen for abnormal blower noise during startup and operation. Do not open the device or perform internal blower service unless authorized and trained.

steps.title: Perform the Approved Preventive Maintenance Procedure
steps.instructions: Complete preventive maintenance using the approved facility checklist and manufacturer-based procedure. Required checks may include visual inspection, electrical safety testing if required, battery condition check, filter replacement, alarm function checks, ventilation performance checks, oxygen-related checks, flow or pressure verification, pre-use check, documentation of parts replaced, and review of service counters.

steps.title: Reset Service Status Only After PM Completion
steps.instructions: Use only the approved reset method according to facility and manufacturer procedure. Do not reset the interval if PM was not completed, the ventilator failed required checks, parts are still needed, or battery, blower, filter, or alarm issues remain unresolved.

steps.title: Run Final Operational Checks
steps.instructions: Power cycle the ventilator. Confirm Alarm 246001 does not return. Run the required pre-use check or self-test. Confirm audible and visual alarms function. Verify external power and battery status. Confirm no new alarms are present.

CCR.Complaint: Hamilton C1 ventilator displayed Alarm 246001 Preventive maintenance required during startup.
CCR.Cause: Scheduled preventive maintenance interval was due. PM history confirmed the device was past its required maintenance date.
CCR.Resolution: Removed ventilator from service, completed required preventive maintenance checklist, inspected filters, verified battery charging, completed operational checks, reset service status per approved procedure, and returned unit to service.

title: Hamilton C1 Ventilator - Alarm: 283009 Self test failed
description: Troubleshooting guide for Hamilton C1 Alarm: Technical Fault: 283009 Self test failed when the ventilator cannot pass startup or pre-use verification and should not be used until the self-test issue is resolved.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot the self-test failure while the ventilator is supporting a patient. Remove the ventilator from use, use another verified ventilator if needed, and label the Hamilton C1 as unavailable until the self-test passes or the issue is resolved.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Power the ventilator on, observe the startup sequence, and confirm the displayed alarm is Alarm: Technical Fault: 283009 Self test failed. Note whether the failure occurs during startup, pre-use check, after circuit setup, after accessory connection, or after a configuration change.

steps.title: Check External Power and Startup Conditions
steps.instructions: Confirm the AC power cord is connected, the wall outlet is functional, the device is not running only on a depleted battery, and the power cord and inlet are not damaged. Repeat the self-test after stable power is confirmed.

steps.title: Inspect the Patient Circuit Setup
steps.instructions: Confirm the correct patient circuit is installed. Inspect inspiratory and expiratory limbs for kinks, occlusions, water, incorrect connections, loose fittings, or damaged tubing. Correct the setup and repeat the self-test.

steps.title: Check the Flow Sensor and Flow Sensor Tubing
steps.instructions: Verify the correct flow sensor is installed and both sensing lines are secure. Check for reversed, kinked, blocked, wet, cracked, or disconnected tubing. Replace damaged or contaminated parts and repeat the self-test.

steps.title: Inspect the Expiratory Valve Assembly
steps.instructions: Inspect the expiratory valve for wrong type, poor seating, missing or damaged membrane, cracks, contamination, moisture, or sticky movement. Reinstall or replace as needed and repeat the self-test.

steps.title: Verify Oxygen Supply
steps.instructions: Check the oxygen wall outlet or cylinder, confirm the oxygen hose is secure, inspect the hose for kinks or damage, verify oxygen is available, and check the oxygen inlet and filter area for obvious blockage.

steps.title: Check Filters and Air Intake Path
steps.instructions: Inspect air inlet filters, vents, and external filter areas for dust buildup, blockage, contamination, fluid exposure, or restricted airflow. Replace filters if dirty, wet, damaged, or overdue.

steps.title: Remove Nonessential Accessories and Repeat the Test
steps.instructions: Disconnect nonessential accessories and optional modules. Use a known-good circuit, flow sensor, and expiratory valve if available. Repeat the self-test to separate accessory-related failures from device-related failures.

steps.title: Repeat the Self-Test After Corrections
steps.instructions: After each correction, reconnect the setup and run the self-test again. If the self-test passes, complete required operational checks. If Alarm: Technical Fault: 283009 Self test failed returns, remove the ventilator from service for repair-level evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: Hamilton C1 ventilator displayed Alarm: Technical Fault: 283009 Self test failed during startup/pre-use check and could not be cleared for patient use.
CCR.Cause: Patient circuit, flow sensor tubing, expiratory valve, oxygen supply, filters, and connected accessories were inspected. Self-test continued to fail after external setup checks, indicating repair-level evaluation was required.
CCR.Resolution: Removed ventilator from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for bench evaluation/repair. If alarm cleared after correcting setup, ventilator passed self-test and required operational checks before return to service.

title: Hamilton C1 Ventilator - Alarm: 244008 Battery 1: Wrong battery
description: Troubleshooting Error 244008 Battery 1: Wrong battery on the Hamilton C1 ventilator when Battery 1 is not recognized as an approved compatible battery and backup power reliability may be affected.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the ventilator from patient use if battery operation may be needed or if backup power reliability cannot be confirmed. Transfer the patient to another ventilator or approved backup method before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm Error 244008 is present and the displayed message reads Battery 1: Wrong battery. Note when the error occurs and whether Battery 2 is recognized normally.

steps.title: Confirm AC Power Is Stable
steps.instructions: Connect the ventilator to a known-good AC outlet, confirm the power cord is seated, and verify AC power indicators are present before evaluating the battery issue.

steps.title: Inspect Battery 1 Installation
steps.instructions: Remove and reinstall Battery 1, checking for poor seating, obstruction, debris, or physical damage in the battery compartment.

steps.title: Verify Battery Compatibility
steps.instructions: Confirm Battery 1 is an approved Hamilton-compatible battery for the C1 by checking facility-approved replacement information, battery label, part number, voltage rating, and physical fit.

steps.title: Swap With a Known-Good Compatible Battery
steps.instructions: Install a known-good approved compatible battery in the Battery 1 position. If the error clears, remove the original battery from service. If the error remains, suspect a device-side issue.

steps.title: Inspect Battery Contacts
steps.instructions: Inspect battery and battery bay contacts for bent pins, corrosion, contamination, fluid residue, or physical damage. Clean only according to facility-approved methods.

steps.title: Check Charging and Recognition Status
steps.instructions: Install the approved battery, connect AC power, and confirm Battery 1 is detected and shows charging or normal charge status.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Issue Is Battery-Related or Device-Related
steps.instructions: Use results from known-good battery testing and Battery 2 comparison to determine whether the issue is isolated to the battery, Battery 1 bay, contacts, or internal power management system.

CCR.Complaint: Hamilton C1 ventilator reported Error 244008 Battery 1: Wrong battery. Battery 1 was not recognized as a compatible battery and backup power reliability could not be confirmed.
CCR.Cause: Battery 1 was not identified by the ventilator as an approved compatible battery. Battery seating, contacts, compatibility, and charging status were checked. Known-good battery testing was used to determine whether the issue was battery-related or device-related.
CCR.Resolution: Installed approved Hamilton-compatible battery in Battery 1 position, inspected contacts, confirmed battery was recognized, and verified charging status. If alarm persisted with known-good battery, ventilator was removed from service and sent for repair evaluation.

title: Hamilton C1 Ventilator - Alarm: 244009 Battery 2: Wrong battery
description: Troubleshooting guide for Alarm 244009 Battery 2: Wrong battery on the Hamilton C1 ventilator, including patient safety, battery compatibility, seating, contacts, charging verification, and escalation when Battery 2 is not recognized.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not remove batteries or interrupt power while the ventilator is supporting a patient. Transfer the patient to another approved ventilator if Battery 2 replacement or power testing could affect ventilation. Confirm external AC power is connected before performing battery checks.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the ventilator displays Alarm 244009 Battery 2: Wrong battery. Observe whether the alarm appears at startup, after inserting Battery 2, during charging, after switching to battery operation, or intermittently during operation.

steps.title: Confirm External Power Is Stable
steps.instructions: Verify the AC power cord, wall outlet, and external power indicator. Test with a known-good power cord or outlet if available so the ventilator remains safely powered while Battery 2 is checked.

steps.title: Inspect Battery 2 Installation
steps.instructions: Remove Battery 2 only when safe. Inspect the battery compartment for poor seating, obstruction, debris, or damage. Reinstall Battery 2 fully and confirm it locks into place.

steps.title: Verify Battery 2 Is an Approved Compatible Battery
steps.instructions: Compare Battery 2 to a known approved Hamilton C1 battery. Check the part number, labeling, battery type, and physical fit. Do not use aftermarket, incorrect, expired, rebuilt, or non-approved batteries.

steps.title: Inspect Battery Contacts
steps.instructions: Check Battery 2 and ventilator-side contacts for dirt, corrosion, bent contacts, damage, residue, or fluid contamination. Clean only according to approved facility and manufacturer cleaning practices.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good Compatible Battery
steps.instructions: Install a known-good approved Hamilton-compatible battery into the Battery 2 slot. If the alarm clears, the original battery is incorrect, defective, or incompatible. If the alarm remains, suspect the Battery 2 bay, contacts, or internal communication hardware.

steps.title: Confirm Recognition and Charging
steps.instructions: Confirm Battery 2 appears in the ventilator battery status display, shows charging or an appropriate charge level, and no battery-related alarms remain. Perform runtime or charge verification if required by facility policy.

steps.title: Check for Physical Damage or Contamination
steps.instructions: Inspect the battery bay and device exterior for cracked plastic, broken latches, loose battery fit, liquid intrusion, corrosion, overheating, abnormal heat, or burning odor.

steps.title: Return to Service Only If Fully Resolved
steps.instructions: Return the ventilator to service only after Battery 2 is approved, properly seated, recognized, charging normally, and the ventilator passes required operational verification.

CCR.Complaint: Hamilton C1 ventilator displayed Alarm 244009 Battery 2: Wrong battery. Staff reported Battery 2 was not recognized correctly and battery status was abnormal.
CCR.Cause: Battery 2 was not an approved compatible battery or was not being identified correctly by the ventilator. Battery seating, contacts, AC power, and compatibility were checked.
CCR.Resolution: Removed incorrect Battery 2 and installed an approved Hamilton-compatible battery. Inspected Battery 2 contacts and battery bay. Confirmed Battery 2 was recognized and charging normally. Alarm cleared and device passed operational verification.

title: Hamilton C1 Ventilator - Alarm: 249020 Invalid option board
description: Troubleshooting guide for Alarm: 249020 Invalid option board on a Hamilton C1 ventilator when the device does not accept an installed option or configuration board.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the ventilator from patient use if the alarm is active, persistent, or affects ventilator startup, configuration, or available features. Transfer the patient to another approved ventilator before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the ventilator displays Alarm: 249020 Invalid option board. Record whether it occurs at startup, during self-test, after service, after a software or configuration change, or intermittently during operation.

steps.title: Check Whether the Alarm Followed Recent Service
steps.instructions: Review whether an option board was installed or replaced, software was updated, configuration was changed, or internal service work was recently completed.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Ventilator Safely
steps.instructions: When the ventilator is not connected to a patient, perform a controlled shutdown and restart. Observe whether Alarm: 249020 returns during startup.

steps.title: Verify External Power and Startup Stability
steps.instructions: Confirm the ventilator is connected to a known-good AC outlet, inspect the power cord, check battery status, and verify the ventilator starts without flickering, resets, or interruptions.

steps.title: Confirm Installed Options and Configuration
steps.instructions: Compare the ventilator's installed options with service records, asset history, or facility configuration documentation. Look for missing, duplicated, or unexpected options.

steps.title: Check for Software and Compatibility Issues
steps.instructions: Review software update history and confirm the installed option hardware is compatible with the ventilator software and configuration.

steps.title: Inspect for Obvious Physical or Service-Related Issues
steps.instructions: Inspect the exterior for impact, liquid intrusion, missing covers, service tampering, or evidence of recent internal access. Do not perform deep internal repair while the device is in clinical service.

steps.title: Repeat Required Startup or Pre-Use Checks
steps.instructions: Restart the ventilator, run the appropriate pre-use check or self-test if available, confirm the alarm does not return, and verify expected options and features are available.

CCR.Complaint: Hamilton C1 ventilator displayed Alarm: 249020 Invalid option board during startup. Staff reported the ventilator would not clear the technical alarm and option configuration appeared unavailable.
CCR.Cause: Checked AC power, startup behavior, recent service history, installed option configuration, and software compatibility. Alarm persisted, indicating likely internal option board, configuration, or board communication fault.
CCR.Resolution: Removed ventilator from patient use, labeled Out of Service, and sent for service-level bench evaluation/vendor repair. Device not returned to service due to unresolved option board alarm.

title: Hamilton C1 Ventilator - Alarm: 285004 Function key not operational
description: Troubleshooting Alarm 285004 Function key not operational on a Hamilton C1 ventilator when a hard key or function key is stuck, damaged, contaminated, or not responding correctly.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot ventilator control problems on an active patient. Remove the ventilator from patient use if the function key issue affects operation, alarm silence, setting changes, ventilation control, or emergency response. Place the patient on another approved ventilator or backup ventilation device if needed.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Check the display for Alarm: 285004 Function key not operational. Confirm whether the alarm appears during startup, normal use, after a button press, intermittently, or continuously. Ask staff which key was affected and whether the issue affects alarm silence, menu navigation, setting changes, or other control functions.

steps.title: Check for a Stuck or Pressed Function Key
steps.instructions: Inspect each hard key and function key. Look for buttons that appear stuck down, lower than others, tilted, cracked, or held by debris. Gently press and release each accessible key to confirm normal travel and return.

steps.title: Check for Physical Damage or Contamination
steps.instructions: Inspect the front panel for cracked keypad overlay, fluid residue, cleaning chemical buildup, torn membrane, sticky button movement, impact damage, or loose panel edges. Clean external contamination only according to approved procedures. Remove the ventilator from service if fluid intrusion or front panel damage is suspected.

steps.title: Confirm the Function Key Response
steps.instructions: Only test the affected key when the ventilator is off patient use. Press the suspected function key and confirm whether the ventilator responds normally. Compare response to nearby keys and check whether the alarm returns after the key is pressed.

steps.title: Check for External Pressure on the Front Panel
steps.instructions: Remove anything touching the front panel. Confirm the ventilator is not pushed tightly against a cart, rail, wall, accessory, or cable bundle. Check that no protective cover, label, tape, or cleaning residue is interfering with the keys.

steps.title: Power Cycle and Recheck
steps.instructions: Only power cycle the ventilator when it is not connected to a patient. Shut down the ventilator using normal procedure, restart it, observe startup, and check whether Alarm 285004 returns. Complete required operational checks before returning the ventilator to service.

CCR.Complaint: Hamilton C1 ventilator reported Alarm 285004 Function key not operational. Staff stated a hard key/function key was not responding correctly during use.
CCR.Cause: Front panel inspected. No external obstruction found. Key response remained abnormal after checking for stuck, damaged, or contaminated buttons. Suspected keypad/front panel hardware fault.
CCR.Resolution: Ventilator removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for bench evaluation/repair. Device not returned to patient use due to unresolved function key alarm.

title: Hamilton G5 Ventilator - Error: TF 5509 Error Servo Signal Active / Inspiratory Valve Not Functioning Correctly
description: Troubleshooting TF 5509 when an active servo error signal indicates possible inspiratory valve malfunction that may compromise controlled gas delivery and safe ventilation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Immediately remove the ventilator from patient use if TF 5509 occurs during active ventilation or setup for patient use. Provide alternative ventilation using an approved backup ventilator or manual ventilation method according to clinical protocol. Do not continue relying on a ventilator with an active, non-silenceable high-priority technical fault.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm that the ventilator displays TF 5509 and note whether the high-priority alarm is active and cannot be silenced. Determine whether the fault occurs during startup, pre-use testing, initiation of ventilation, intermittent operation, or after restart. Check the event log for TF 5509 and related technical faults.

steps.title: Inspect the Ventilator Externally
steps.instructions: With the device removed from patient use, inspect the housing, gas inlet area, and external gas connections for damage, contamination, loose connections, unusual sounds, odor, or excessive heat.

steps.title: Confirm Power and Gas Supply Conditions
steps.instructions: Connect the ventilator to a known-good AC power source and verify normal power-up behavior. Confirm secure air and oxygen supply connections and appropriate source pressures according to facility and manufacturer requirements.

steps.title: Check Patient Circuit and External Accessories
steps.instructions: Inspect the circuit for obstruction, incorrect assembly, excessive condensation, or incompatible accessories. Install a known-good test circuit and approved test lung or test setup before running functional checks.

steps.title: Review the Event Log
steps.instructions: Document the date and time of TF 5509 occurrences, whether the fault is repeated, any associated technical faults, and whether the error occurred during startup, testing, or ventilation.

steps.title: Perform Inspiratory Valve Checks in Test Mode
steps.instructions: With the ventilator removed from patient care and connected to an approved test setup, perform the Inspiratory Valve Checks in Test Mode according to the Hamilton service procedure available to qualified Clinical Engineering personnel. Confirm the test completes successfully without recurrence of TF 5509 or related alarms.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Issue Is Resolved
steps.instructions: Return the ventilator to service only if TF 5509 does not recur, inspiratory valve checks pass, the event log has been reviewed, and required functional verification confirms safe operation. If the error remains, returns, or testing fails, remove the ventilator from service and route it for repair evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: Hamilton G5 ventilator displayed Error TF 5509 with a non-silenceable high-priority alarm. Fault indicates Error Servo signal remained active, suggesting inspiratory valve or servo malfunction.
CCR.Cause: Verified TF 5509 on display and reviewed event log. External inspection, power source, gas supply connections, and test circuit did not reveal an external cause. Inspiratory valve checks in Test Mode failed or fault recurred, indicating likely internal inspiratory valve or servo failure.
CCR.Resolution: Removed ventilator from clinical service, labeled unit Out of Service, documented event log findings, and routed device for qualified repair evaluation and inspiratory valve replacement if confirmed.

title: Hamilton G5 Ventilator - Error: TF 9306 Inspiratory Valve Leak Higher Than Acceptable
description: Troubleshooting Error: TF 9306 on the Hamilton G5 ventilator when excessive inspiratory valve leakage causes a high-priority alarm, entry into ambient state, and loss of dependable ventilatory support.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Immediately remove the Hamilton G5 from patient use if TF 9306 occurs during ventilation or pre-use setup. Provide alternative ventilation using an approved backup ventilator or manual ventilation method according to clinical protocol. Do not troubleshoot the ventilator while it is supporting an active patient.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm that the ventilator displays Error: TF 9306 Inspiratory Valve Leak Higher Than Acceptable. Determine whether the fault occurs during startup, pre-use testing, attempted ventilation, or a tightness test. Confirm the high-priority alarm, ambient state behavior, and event log entry.

steps.title: Inspect the Breathing Circuit and External Connections
steps.instructions: Inspect accessible circuit connections, tubing, test lung or occlusion device, seals, and connected accessories for looseness, damage, contamination, moisture, or improper assembly. Correct external issues and repeat the applicable pre-use or tightness test.

steps.title: Confirm the Correct Test Setup and Configuration
steps.instructions: Verify that the correct patient circuit type and required external components are installed and seated properly. Confirm the setup matches facility procedure and manufacturer instructions before repeating testing.

steps.title: Examine the Device for Visible External Conditions
steps.instructions: Inspect accessible gas pathway connections and ports for damage, contamination, fluid intrusion, impact evidence, or unusual noise during testing. Document relevant findings and do not disassemble internal valve assemblies during initial troubleshooting.

steps.title: Perform Inspiratory Valve Checks in Test Mode
steps.instructions: With the ventilator off the patient and connected to an approved test setup, perform the Inspiratory Valve Checks in Test Mode according to the manufacturer service procedure. Record whether the inspiratory valve checks and tightness test pass or continue to confirm excessive leakage.

steps.title: Review the Event Log and Determine Final Status
steps.instructions: Review event log occurrences of TF 9306 and associated technical faults. If testing passes after correction of an external cause, complete required operational verification before returning the ventilator to service.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: Confirm TF 9306 is no longer active, verify no high-priority technical alarm remains, complete functional verification, document corrective actions and test results, and return the unit to service only after all required checks pass.

CCR.Complaint: Hamilton G5 ventilator displayed Error: TF 9306 Inspiratory Valve Leak Higher Than Acceptable, generated a high-priority alarm, and entered ambient state during testing or attempted operation.
CCR.Cause: External breathing circuit, accessories, and test setup were inspected and confirmed secure. Inspiratory Valve Checks in Test Mode and the tightness test continued to fail, indicating persistent excessive inspiratory valve leakage consistent with an internal ventilator fault.
CCR.Resolution: Removed Hamilton G5 ventilator from service, labeled unit Out of Service, documented TF 9306 and test results, and routed unit for bench evaluation or manufacturer-authorized repair. Unit was not returned to clinical use.

title: Hamilton G5 Ventilator - Error: TF 5505 Tank pressure above 550 mbar indicating a leaking mixer valve
description: Troubleshooting TF 5505 when excessive tank pressure indicates a suspected leaking mixer valve, activates a non-silenceable high-priority alarm, and causes the ventilator to enter ambient state.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Immediately provide alternative ventilation using an approved backup ventilator or manual ventilation method according to clinical protocol. Do not continue relying on the Hamilton G5 after TF 5505 occurs, and do not troubleshoot while the device is connected to an active patient.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm that the ventilator displays Error: TF 5505. Determine whether the fault occurred during startup, pre-use testing, or attempted ventilation. Confirm whether the high-priority alarm is active and cannot be silenced and whether the ventilator entered ambient state.

steps.title: Inspect Power, Gas Sources, and External Connections
steps.instructions: Confirm stable AC power. Verify that air and oxygen hoses are correctly connected and are not crossed, kinked, visibly damaged, or contaminated. Inspect facility gas connections and inlet fittings for secure attachment, damage, moisture, debris, or contamination.

steps.title: Inspect for Visible Damage or Environmental Factors
steps.instructions: Examine the ventilator exterior and gas inlet area for impact damage, liquid ingress, contamination, or transport damage. Document any unusual sound, odor, or heat reported when the fault occurred.

steps.title: Review the Event Log
steps.instructions: Review the event log according to facility procedure. Confirm that TF 5505 was recorded, determine whether it occurred once or repeatedly, and look for related mixer, pressure, gas supply, or other technical faults.

steps.title: Perform Approved Mixer Calibration and Checks
steps.instructions: With the ventilator removed from patient use, perform the approved Mixer Calibration & Checks in Test Mode according to authorized Hamilton service documentation and facility policy. Document whether testing passes, fails, or causes TF 5505 to recur.

steps.title: Confirm Final Device Status
steps.instructions: Return the ventilator to service only if approved testing passes, TF 5505 does not recur, and all required operational verification is completed according to facility policy. If the fault persists or testing fails, remove the device from service, label it Out of Service, and send it for authorized repair or bench evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: Hamilton G5 ventilator displayed Error: TF 5505, sounded a non-silenceable high-priority alarm, and entered ambient state during startup or attempted operation.
CCR.Cause: Event log confirmed TF 5505 indicating tank pressure above 550 mbar and suspected leaking mixer valve. External gas connections and visible condition were checked with no correctable external cause identified. Approved Mixer Calibration & Checks failed or the fault recurred.
CCR.Resolution: Removed ventilator from service, labeled unit Out of Service, documented event log and test findings, and routed unit for authorized bench evaluation and repair due to unresolved TF 5505 mixer pressure fault.

title: Hamilton G5 Ventilator - Error: TF 5506 Tank Pressure Too High / Mixer Valve Leak Indicated
description: Troubleshooting TF 5506 when excessive internal tank pressure indicates a possible mixer valve leak, activates a non-silenceable high-priority alarm, and causes the ventilator to enter ambient state.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Immediately provide alternative ventilation using an approved ventilator or manual resuscitation method directed by clinical staff. Do not rely on the Hamilton G5 after TF 5506 occurs because the ventilator enters ambient state and the high-priority alarm cannot be silenced.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm Error: TF 5506 is displayed. Verify that the high-priority alarm is active and cannot be silenced, the ventilator enters ambient state, and the fault is recorded in the event log.

steps.title: Confirm the Ventilator Is Removed From Clinical Use
steps.instructions: Disconnect the ventilator from patient use, place it in a safe troubleshooting area, and apply an Out of Service label if the fault occurred during clinical use or remains unresolved.

steps.title: Check External Gas Supply Conditions
steps.instructions: Inspect air and oxygen hose connections, gas supply condition, cylinder regulators if used, and gas inlet connections for contamination, moisture, damage, kinks, or abnormal supply concerns. Use known-good approved gas supplies before retesting.

steps.title: Inspect the Device Exterior and Gas Inlets
steps.instructions: Check for damaged gas inlet fittings, contamination, liquid intrusion, impact damage, abnormal sound, odor, heat, or loose external connections. Stop testing and escalate if an unsafe physical condition is present.

steps.title: Review the Event Log
steps.instructions: Review the event log according to approved Clinical Engineering procedures. Confirm TF 5506 is recorded and check for recurring faults or related mixer, pressure, gas supply, or sensor errors.

steps.title: Perform Mixer Calibration & Checks in Test Mode
steps.instructions: With the ventilator removed from patient use, perform the approved Mixer Calibration & Checks in Test Mode and observe whether TF 5506 recurs or testing fails.

steps.title: Interpret the Test Result
steps.instructions: If testing passes without recurrence, complete required functional verification before returning the ventilator to service. If TF 5506 returns or testing fails, keep the ventilator out of service for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: Return the ventilator to service only after approved testing passes, the event log is reviewed, no fault recurs, and all required post-service functional checks are completed and documented.

CCR.Complaint: Hamilton G5 ventilator displayed Error: TF 5506 with non-silenceable high-priority alarm and entered ambient state during operation or pre-use testing. Device unavailable for controlled ventilation.
CCR.Cause: Verified TF 5506 in event log. External gas connections and inlet condition checked with no external cause identified. Fault indicates excessive internal tank pressure consistent with a leaking mixer valve or related sensor board fault.
CCR.Resolution: Removed ventilator from service and labeled Out of Service. Confirmed alternative ventilation prior to troubleshooting. Performed Mixer Calibration & Checks in Test Mode and reviewed event log. Unit referred for bench repair evaluation and mixer valve or sensor board replacement as indicated.

title: Hamilton G5 Ventilator - Error: TF 5507 Air or Oxygen Inlet Valve Cannot Close Properly
description: Troubleshooting TF 5507 when an air or oxygen inlet valve cannot close properly, causing a non-silenceable high-priority alarm, interrupted ventilation, and entry into ambient state.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Immediately provide alternative ventilation using an approved backup ventilator or manual ventilation method according to clinical protocol. Do not continue relying on the Hamilton G5 after TF 5507 occurs because the ventilator enters ambient state and is not providing normal ventilatory support.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm that the ventilator displays TF 5507. Verify whether a non-silenceable high-priority alarm occurred, the technical fault number was displayed, the ventilator entered ambient state, and ventilation was interrupted or unavailable. Review the event log for the time, frequency, and conditions associated with the fault.

steps.title: Remove the Ventilator From Clinical Use
steps.instructions: After the patient is transitioned to alternate ventilation, remove the ventilator from clinical use, label it Out of Service, and do not return it to service simply because the alarm clears after restart.

steps.title: Check External Gas Supply Conditions
steps.instructions: Inspect external air and oxygen supply hoses, connections, wall outlets, cylinder regulators, and available gas pressure. Confirm hoses are connected to the correct inlets, fully seated, undamaged, and free of obvious kinks, contamination, or damaged fittings.

steps.title: Inspect the Ventilator Gas Inlet Area Externally
steps.instructions: Examine the air and oxygen inlet fittings for visible damage, debris, contamination, fluid intrusion, bent connectors, looseness, or transport-related damage. Do not perform deep internal disassembly during initial troubleshooting.

steps.title: Confirm Whether Correcting an External Issue Clears the Error
steps.instructions: If an external gas supply, hose, or inlet connection issue was corrected, perform a controlled device check away from patient use. Confirm TF 5507 does not recur, startup completes normally, no high-priority technical fault appears, and the ventilator does not enter ambient state.

steps.title: Perform Mixer Calibration and Checks in Test Mode
steps.instructions: If external gas sources, hoses, and inlet connections are acceptable, perform the manufacturer-specified Mixer Calibration & Checks in Test Mode. Determine whether the test passes, reproduces TF 5507, or identifies an air or oxygen valve control problem.

steps.title: Review the Event Log
steps.instructions: Check the event log for TF 5507 occurrences, repeated or intermittent behavior, related mixer or pressure faults, gas supply faults, startup faults, and timing in relation to testing or clinical use.

steps.title: Determine Whether Repair Escalation Is Required
steps.instructions: If TF 5507 remains present, returns during testing, or Mixer Calibration & Checks fail, keep the ventilator out of service and send it for qualified bench evaluation. Repair may involve the mixer valves or sensor board as indicated by testing.

CCR.Complaint: Hamilton G5 ventilator displayed TF 5507 — air or oxygen inlet valve cannot close properly. Unit generated a non-silenceable high-priority alarm and entered ambient state during startup or operation.
CCR.Cause: Verified external air and oxygen supply hoses and connections were properly seated with no visible damage or contamination. Fault remained present or recurred during controlled testing. Mixer Calibration & Checks indicated a persistent mixer or inlet valve control fault consistent with likely internal mixer valve or sensor board failure.
CCR.Resolution: Removed ventilator from service and labeled Out of Service due to loss of normal ventilation capability and entry into ambient state. Reviewed event log and routed unit for qualified bench evaluation and repair of the mixer valve system or sensor board as indicated.

title: Hamilton G5 Ventilator - Error: TF 5510 dP Sensor Signal Above Limit; Autozero Valve Switching Failure
description: Troubleshooting Error TF 5510 when the dP Sensor signal exceeds its expected limit and suggests autozero valve switching failure while the ventilator may continue ventilation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Notify clinical staff and move the patient to an approved alternative ventilator before troubleshooting. Do not rely on continued ventilation from a device reporting an unresolved technical fault.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm Error TF 5510 on the display, identify whether it occurs at startup or during ventilation, note whether it is intermittent or repeatable, and review the event log for associated fault records.

steps.title: Confirm the Device Is Appropriate for Bench Evaluation
steps.instructions: Remove the ventilator from patient use and apply an Out of Service label if the error remains active or returns. Inspect externally for impact, contamination, damaged accessible connections, abnormal noise, odor, heat, or additional faults.

steps.title: Check Power and Startup Behavior
